```{r include=FALSE}
                                              'unemployment.total.yoy')

#make list of datasets to merge
dataset_list <- list(H2_unemployment, H1_AMECO_cyc_adj, H2_labor_productivity, H2_output_gap)

H2_final <- Reduce(function(x, y) merge(x, y, by = c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD"), all = TRUE), 
                   dataset_list)

nrow(H2_final)
#209 rows
table(H2_final$geo)
#all countries included with 19 observations each
```

## HYPOTHESIS 2 PREDICTOR LAGS

Similar to H1, I will be lagging all predictors by 1 year. I will lag
both of the cyclically adjusted predictors because they measure deliberate policy
changes outside the business cycle, so by lagging 1 year, the response variable
at time t will be influenced by policies from t-1, which would show clear causation.
Lagging the cyclically adjusted expenditure and revenue changes ensures that
discretionary fiscal policy strictly precedes the labor market response, allowing
the model to capture clear causal effects.

YoY change in the output gap is lagged by one year to capture the cyclical baseline
of the economy entering the period. If current-period output gap t were used, it
would suffer from severe simultaneity bias; an immediate spike in unemployment
at time t would inherently alter aggregate demand and skew the simultaneous
output gap. Lagging it controls for the pre-existing economic momentum without
letting current labor market shocks corrupt the control.

YoY change in labor productivity growth is lagged to establish the structural health 
and technological resilience of a country's workforce prior to fiscal adjustments.
If a country was already experiencing stagnant productivity growth at t-1, it
inherited a weaker economic cushion, making firms more prone to immediate layoffs
when austerity hit. Using the lagged value prevents current-year mass layoffs
(which can artificially spike measured productivity per remaining worker at time t)
from creating reverse causality.

While my dependent variable is the change in the unemployment rate (which naturally
reduces autocorrelation), my core predictors are scaled as a percentage of GDP.
Lagging these predictors ensures that sudden, unobserved macroeconomic shocks—or
massive accounting revisions in GDP cannot distort the response variable.

```{r include=FALSE}
#ensure that rows are in the exact linear sequence required for lagging.
H2_final <- H2_final[order(H2_final$geo, H2_final$TIME_PERIOD), ]

#drop the last observation of a vector and shift everything down by one
lag <- function(x) {return(c(NA, x[-length(x)]))}

#apply the lag to every country and create new columns
H2_final$lag.yoy.AMECO.rev <- ave(H2_final$AMECO.rev.yoy, H2_final$geo, FUN = lag)
H2_final$lag.yoy.AMECO.exp <- ave(H2_final$AMECO.exp.yoy, H2_final$geo, FUN = lag)
H2_final$lag.yoy.output   <- ave(H2_final$output.yoy.value, H2_final$geo, FUN = lag)
H2_final$lag.yoy.labor  <- ave(H2_final$labor.obs.value, H2_final$geo, FUN = lag)

#check it worked
verification_fields <- c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD", "output.yoy.value", "lag.yoy.output", 
                         "labor.obs.value", "lag.yoy.labor")

print(H2_final[60:80, verification_fields], row.names = FALSE)
#everything looks good
```

## HYPOTHESIS 2 EXCLUDING IRELAND TOTAL UNEMPLOYMENT ASSUMPTION CHECKS

```{r include=FALSE}
#remove years 2001 and 2002 because contains NA values from YoY and lag calcs
H2_final <- H2_final[H2_final$TIME_PERIOD != "2001" & H2_final$TIME_PERIOD != '2002', ]
sum(is.na(H2_final))
table(H2_final$geo)
#no missing values and 17 observations per country
#dataset excluding Ireland because of 2015 accounting shift
H2_final_no_ireland <- subset(H2_final, geo != "Ireland")
```

Our Hypothesis 2 model

```{r echo=TRUE}
H2_plm_no_ireland <- plm(unemployment.total.yoy ~ lag.yoy.AMECO.rev + lag.yoy.AMECO.exp + 
                                 lag.yoy.labor + lag.yoy.output,
                         data = H2_final_no_ireland,
                         index = c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD"),
                         model = "within", #within = fixed effects
                         effect = "twoways")
```

### MULTICOLLINEARITY CHECK

```{r include=FALSE}
#extract the within-transformed (demeaned) variables properly
transformed_X <- as.data.frame(model.matrix(H2_plm_no_ireland))
transformed_Y <- as.numeric(pmodel.response(H2_plm_no_ireland))

#bind them together into a temporary data frame
vif_data <- cbind(transformed_Y, transformed_X)

#run standard lm() because R now sees them as completely separate columns
pure_vif_check <- lm(transformed_Y ~ ., data = vif_data)
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
vif(pure_vif_check)
# > vif(pure_vif_check)
# lag.yoy.AMECO.rev lag.yoy.AMECO.exp     lag.yoy.labor    lag.yoy.output 
# 1.203651          1.126422          1.411148          1.443360
```

All predictors have very low multicollinearity. This was done by treating 
the VIF check the same way the plm model handles the data, resulting in the 
isolated variations in each predictor.

### LINEARITY CHECK

```{r include=FALSE}
#extract the clean, model-transformed dependent variable (excludes dropped rows)
actual_Y <- as.numeric(pmodel.response(H2_plm_no_ireland))

#extract the model residuals
resids <- as.numeric(residuals(H2_plm_no_ireland))

#calculate fitted values using matching dimensions
fitted_values <- actual_Y - resids
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
#plot residual vs fitted values to check for linearity
plot(fitted_values, resids, 
     main = "H2 Total Unemployment ex Ireland - Residuals vs Fitted", 
     xlab = "Fitted Values", ylab = "Residuals",
     pch = 19, col = rgb(0, 0, 0, 0.5))
abline(h = 0, col = "red", lwd = 2)

#LOESS smooth trend line (Blue, solid
lines(lowess(fitted_values, resids, f=0.75), col = "blue", lwd = 2)
```

All residuals are evenly scattered above and below 0. Our blue LOESS trend line is 
straight with no curve and stays close to 0 throughout the bulk of the points. 
There is no shape or pattern in the residuals and they appear randomly scattered. 
Our LOESS line does move upward from the large fitted, high residual points
beyond the +1.5 fitted value mark, given that these are the only points influencing
the trend line on the far right side. Since the LOESS line is straight and close
to 0 in the bulk of the randomly scattered points, I will say linearity assumption
is met.

For homoscedasticity, there are some residuals that have much higher variance, 
so homoscedasticity may not be met. Most residuals do fall between -1.5 and 1.5,
with only a small amount of residuals greater than these values. I will do a formal
Breusch-Pagan test to check homoscedasticity, and Driscoll-Kraay standard errors will be
applied regardless of the result to further protect the models standard errors.
As for the spread on the x-axis, there are some outlier fitted values on the right 
and left, but it is only a handful of values. I will create a residual vs leverage
plot to make sure these points are not strongly influencing my regression model.
Overall, I will say linearity is met.

### INDEPENDENCE CHECK

Test for Autocorrelation within countries
H0: errors are independent (no autocorrelation)
H1: errors are not independent (autocorrelation exists)

```{r echo=FALSE}
pbgtest(H2_plm_no_ireland) #Breusch-Godfrey test for panel data
# > pbgtest(H2_plm_no_ireland) #Breusch-Godfrey test for panel data
# 
# Breusch-Godfrey/Wooldridge test for serial correlation in panel models
# 
# data:  unemployment.total.yoy ~ lag.yoy.AMECO.rev + lag.yoy.AMECO.exp +  ...
# chisq = 51.036, df = 17, p-value = 2.913e-05
# alternative hypothesis: serial correlation in idiosyncratic errors
```

With a significance level of 0.05 and a p-value of 2.913e-05, we reject H0 and 
conclude that our errors are not independent and suffer from autocorrelation

Test for Cross-Sectional Dependence (Spatial correlation between countries)
H0: errors are independent
H1: errors are not independent

```{r echo=FALSE}
pcdtest(H2_plm_no_ireland, test = "lm")
# > pcdtest(H2_plm_no_ireland, test = "lm")
# 
# Breusch-Pagan LM test for cross-sectional dependence in panels
# 
# data:  unemployment.total.yoy ~ lag.yoy.AMECO.rev + lag.yoy.AMECO.exp + lag.yoy.labor + lag.yoy.output
# chisq = 77.001, df = 45, p-value = 0.002085
# alternative hypothesis: cross-sectional dependence
```

With a significance level of 0.05 and a p-value of 0.002085, we reject H0 and 
conclude that errors are not independent between countries.

Since autocorrelation is present within countries and between countries,
we apply Driscoll-Kraay standard errors. 

### NORMALITY CHECK

```{r echo=FALSE}
#extract clean numeric residuals
resids <- as.numeric(residuals(H2_plm_no_ireland))

qqnorm(resids, main = "H2 Total Unemployment ex Ireland - QQ Plot of Panel Residuals",
       ylab='standardized residuals')
qqline(resids, col = "red")
```

Our QQ plot shows that the panel residuals track the theoretical normal 
distribution closely between the -1.5 to 1.5 quantiles. Beyond this, the plot 
shows heavy tails on both ends, indicating large residuals that deviate from a 
strict normal distribution. The heavy-tails are expected given the macroeconomic 
impacts during the crisis on unemployment rates. However, with a total 
panel size of 170 observations, the Central Limit Theorem ensures that the distribution
of our coefficient estimates is asymptotically normal. Therefore, despite the non-normal
distributions present in the extreme tails of the raw residuals, we can confidently
rely on our standard errors and final inferential results. 
Overall, while the strict assumption of residual normality is technically violated
in the tails due to predictable crisis-driven outliers, the asymptotic properties 
of our sample size render the model highly robust and statistically valid. 

### EQUAL VARIANCE CHECK

H0: errors have equal variance
H1: errors do not have equal variance

```{r echo=FALSE}
bptest(H2_plm_no_ireland)
# > bptest(H2_plm_no_ireland)
# 
# studentized Breusch-Pagan test
# 
# data:  H2_plm_no_ireland
# BP = 30.841, df = 4, p-value = 3.299e-06
```

With a significance level of 0.05 and p-value of 3.299e-06, we reject H0 and 
conclude that our errors do not have equal variance. This is expected
because out 11 countries are not all uniform. A 1% increase in fiscal tightening
can cause predictable responses in one country but highly volatile responses in 
another. Furthermore, the impact of the crisis was highly unequal among the
included nations, with the peripheral countries ultimately receiving the most economic
impact relative to the core Eurozone countries.

Because the data has already been transformed via a Two-Way Within specification
to resolve fixed effect dummy bloat, we can apply non-parametric covariance 
adjustments directly to the active variations. We implement Driscoll-Kraay (SCC) 
standard errors. This framework is uniquely suited to address the Breusch-Pagan 
violation because it modifies the variance-covariance matrix to be entirely robust
to heteroscedasticity. Furthermore, it simultaneously corrects for spatial
cross-sectional dependence (the tendency of Eurozone labor markets to move together
due to shared monetary policy) and temporal autocorrelation up to a designated
lag length (t-2). By mapping the Driscoll-Kraay correction onto the demeaning 
framework, the uneven variance identified by the Breusch-Pagan test is entirely 
absorbed. This ensures that the final significance testing and confidence intervals
for our expenditure and revenue predictors are statistically conservative.

### LEVERAGE AND COOK'S DISTANCE CHECK EX IRELAND

```{r include=FALSE}
#reset row names to sequence from 1 to the total number of rows
rownames(H2_final_no_ireland) <- NULL
#use least squares dummy variable 
H2_lsdv <- lm(unemployment.total.yoy ~ lag.yoy.AMECO.rev + lag.yoy.AMECO.exp +
                lag.yoy.output + lag.yoy.labor + 
                factor(geo) + factor(TIME_PERIOD), data = H2_final_no_ireland)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
#Residuals vs Leverage plot
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
plot(H2_lsdv, which = 5, main = "H2 Total Unemployment ex Ireland - Residuals vs Leverage")

#Cook's Distance by Observation Index
cooks_vals <- cooks.distance(H2_lsdv)
plot(cooks_vals, type = "h", main = "Cook's Distance", ylab = "Cook's D Value")

# Add a horizontal line for the 4/n threshold
abline(h = 4 / nrow(H2_final_no_ireland), col = "red", lty = 2)

#find the country and year for our top leverage points
H2_final_no_ireland[c(42, 93, 160), c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD")]
# > H2_final_no_ireland[c(42, 93, 160), c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD")]
# geo TIME_PERIOD
# 42  Finland        2010
# 93   Greece        2010
# 160   Spain        2009
```

The diagnostics highlight three critical observations—Finland 2010, Greece 2010,
and Spain 2009—that exert substantial influence over the model's structure.
Observation 160 (Spain, 2009): is a high-leverage, high-residual outlier, displaying
a large positive standardized residual of approximately +3.8 and leverage approaching
0.4. This point captures the immediate labor market shock caused by the collapse
of the Spanish housing bubble.

Observation 93 (Greece, 2010): is a positive standardized residual of approximately +2.5
and moderate leverage, representing the initial spike in Greek unemployment as
the sovereign debt crisis began to manifest.

Observation 42 (Finland, 2010): is a significant negative-residual outlier
(approximately -3.5) with leverage around 0.3, reflecting idiosyncratic labor market
dynamics during the period of post-crisis recovery.

On the Cook's Distance plot, while the vast majority of the 11-country cohort
remains stable and well-behaved below the sample-size adjusted heuristic limit
of 4/n (approximately 0.02), these three observations form a clear cluster of
systemic volatility. Observation 160 (Spain, 2009) registers the absolute highest
Cook's D value in the sample, spiking to approximately 0.30, while Observation
93 (Greece, 2010) follows with a Cook's D value near 0.15. Although these points
do not reach the conservative 1.0 threshold on the Residuals vs. Leverage plot,
they exert a disproportionate influence over the final beta estimates, serving
as the primary drivers of volatility in the model for the unemployment hypothesis.

Despite their high leverage and substantial Cook's Distance values, these observations
are intentionally retained in the baseline model. The observations for Spain,
Greece, and Finland represent genuine macroeconomic reality. Removing these
points would introduce severe selection bias, effectively stripping the dataset
of the very labor market shocks—such as housing bubble bursts and rapid unemployment
spikes during austerity—that the study aims to analyze. Excluding these data points
would artificially smooth the panel, understate the true systemic risks associated
with aggressive fiscal consolidation, and yield a model that fails to capture
the core historical realities of the Eurozone's labor market deterioration.

## HYPOTHESIS 2 EXCLUDING IRELAND TOTAL UNEMPLOYMENT ANALYSIS

```{r include=FALSE}
#apply Driscoll-Kraay Standard Errors to H2 plm model
H2_final_results <- coeftest(H2_plm_no_ireland, 
                             vcov = vcovSCC(H2_plm_no_ireland, 
                                            type = "HC3", 
                                            cluster = "time"))
```

Null Hypothesis 2: Holding the total size of fiscal consolidation constant (budget deficit 
reduction of the exact same size), the impact of cyclically adjusted expenditure
cuts on the national unemployment rate is equal to the impact of cyclically adjusted
revenue increases. (The specific mix of austerity has no unique relationship with
job losses) (beta_exp = beta_rev).

Alternative Hypothesis 2: The impact of cyclically adjusted expenditure cuts on the 
national unemployment rate is significantly different from the impact of cyclically 
adjusted revenue increases. (Shifting the balance toward spending cuts changes how 
many jobs are lost during consolidation) (beta_exp != beta_rev).

```{r echo=FALSE}
print(H2_final_results)
# > print(H2_final_results)
# 
# t test of coefficients:
#   
#                     Estimate Std. Error  t value  Pr(>|t|)    
# lag.yoy.AMECO.rev -0.272970   0.211962  -1.2878  0.199931    
# lag.yoy.AMECO.exp  0.065794   0.031477   2.0902  0.038404 *  
#   lag.yoy.labor      0.157503   0.058090   2.7113  0.007541 ** 
#   lag.yoy.output    -0.748814   0.060698 -12.3368 < 2.2e-16 ***
#   ---
#   Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1
```

Lagged Cyclically Adjusted Revenues (lag.yoy.AMECO.rev):
The Coefficient is -0.2730 with a p-value of p = 0.200. Holding all else constant,
a 1 percentage point increase in the lagged YoY change of cyclically adjusted revenues 
(revenue-based consolidation/tax hikes) is associated with a 0.273 percentage point 
decrease in the national unemployment rate change the following year.
The high p-value is above our significance level of 0.05, so we fail to reject H0 
and conclude that revenue-based policy changes in year t-1 have a statistically 
insignificant relationship with the total unemployment rate trajectory in the following 
year t. From a policy standpoint, increasing or decreasing structural revenues does 
not reliably push or pull unemployment growth deviations up or down in the following year.

Lagged Cyclically Adjusted Expenditures (lag.yoy.AMECO.exp):
The Coefficient is 0.0658 with a p-value of p = 0.038. Holding all else constant,
a 1 percentage point increase in the lagged YoY change of cyclically adjusted 
expenditures is associated with a 0.066 percentage point increase in the national
unemployment rate change the following year. The p-value is below our significance 
level of 0.05, so we reject H0 and conclude that expenditure-based changes in year 
t-1 have a statistically significant impact on the change in the total unemployment 
rate in the following year t. Because a spending cut represents a negative change 
in expenditures, this positive coefficient mathematically demonstrates that a 1 
percentage point decrease in expenditures leads to a subsequent drop in the unemployment
change vector by 0.066 percentage points. This indicates that the total unemployment 
rate trajectory at time t is impacted differently from a 1 percentage point increase in
cyclically adjusted revenues versus expenditures at t-1, providing empirical proof 
that spending-based austerity adjustments behave distinctly from tax interventions.

Lagged Labor Productivity Growth (lag.yoy.labor):
The Coefficient is 0.1575 with a p-value of p = 0.008. Holding all else constant, 
a 1 percentage point increase in the lagged YoY change of real labor productivity 
per hour worked is associated with a 0.158 percentage point increase in the national
unemployment rate change in year t. The purpose of this coefficient is to establish 
the structural health and technological resilience of a country's workforce prior
to fiscal adjustments, ensuring our austerity coefficients are not biased by pre-existing 
labor decay. Because it is highly statistically significant (p < 0.01), it suggests 
that after accounting for country and year fixed effects, accelerating workforce 
productivity trends act as a strong independent driver of upward shifts in the 
unemployment rate change, potentially capturing structural labor-shedding dynamics.

Lagged Change in Output Gap (lag.yoy.output):
The Coefficient is -0.7488 with a p-value of p < 0.001. Holding all else constant, 
a 1 percentage point increase in the lagged change of the output gap is associated 
with a 0.749 percentage point decrease in the national unemployment rate change in 
the following year t. The purpose of this variable is to control for the cyclical
position and momentum of the economy entering the fiscal period. Mirroring macroeconomic
expectations (Okun's Law variations), this relationship is extraordinarily statistically
significant (p < 0.001), proving that an expanding economic cycle (closing a negative 
output gap or expanding a positive one) at t-1 strongly decelerates subsequent
unemployment pressures within this two-way demeaning framework.

Formal Wald test whether spending cuts and tax hikes have equal impacts on unemployment

```{r include=FALSE}
H2_hypothesis_test <- linearHypothesis(H2_plm_no_ireland, 
                                       "lag.yoy.AMECO.rev = lag.yoy.AMECO.exp", 
                                       vcov = vcovSCC(H2_plm_no_ireland, 
                                                      type = "HC3", 
                                                      cluster = "time"))
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
print(H2_hypothesis_test)
# > print(H2_hypothesis_test)
# 
# Linear hypothesis test:
#   lag.yoy.AMECO.rev - lag.yoy.AMECO.exp = 0
# 
# Model 1: restricted model
# Model 2: unemployment.total.yoy ~ lag.yoy.AMECO.rev + lag.yoy.AMECO.exp + 
#   lag.yoy.labor + lag.yoy.output
# 
# Note: Coefficient covariance matrix supplied.
# 
# Res.Df Df  Chisq Pr(>Chisq)
# 1    141                     
# 2    140  1 2.1362     0.1439
```

OVERALL HYPOTHESIS 2 CONCLUSION FOR TOTAL UNEMPLOYMENT EX IRELAND:
While the individual coefficient testing showed a significant positive relationship
for structural expenditures (p = 0.038) and an insignificant relationship for
structural revenues (p = 0.200), a formal Linear Restriction Test (Wald Test) was
run to directly test the hypothesis lag.yoy.AMECO.rev - lag.yoy.AMECO.exp = 0.
Utilizing a Driscoll-Kraay robust covariance matrix with HC3 corrections to adjust
for spatial and cross-sectional dependence, the test yields a Chi-squared statistic
of 2.1362 with a p-value of 0.1439. Because this p-value is greater than our 0.05
significance threshold, we fail to reject Null Hypothesis 2.Therefore, we cannot
definitively conclude that the macroeconomic impact on the total unemployment rate
of an expenditure cut is significantly different from the impact of a revenue increase
of the exact same size. The standard error for lag.yoy.AMECO.rev was noticeably
large (0.2120) relative to its estimate (-0.2730). Because the model is uncertain
about the true value of the revenue coefficient, its confidence interval is wide.
That wide interval overlaps with the tighter confidence interval of our expenditure
coefficient (estimate of 0.0658, standard error of 0.0315), preventing a clean statistical
separation. Furthermore, slicing a panel model down by removing Ireland and applying
two-way fixed effects strips out a massive amount of variation and helps eliminate bias.
However, with N=10 countries and a relatively short time series, the Wald test demands
a high threshold of structural divergence to declare two policy coefficients statistically
different from one another, hence we fail to reject H0 at the standard threshold.

## HYPOTHESIS 2 TOTAL UNEMPLOYMENT INCLUDING IRELAND ANALYSIS

Now we will re-run the model with Ireland included in the dataset. This is valuable
because it helps ensure the structural integrity of the model and verifies that 
the findings were not being arbitrarily driven by sample selection.

```{r echo=TRUE}
H2_plm_full_total <- plm(unemployment.total.yoy ~ lag.yoy.AMECO.rev + lag.yoy.AMECO.exp + 
                           lag.yoy.labor + lag.yoy.output,
                   data = H2_final,
                   index = c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD"),
                   model = "within", #within = fixed effects
                   effect = "twoways")
```

```{r include=FALSE}
#extract the within-transformed (demeaned) variables properly
transformed_X <- as.data.frame(model.matrix(H2_plm_full_total))
transformed_Y <- as.numeric(pmodel.response(H2_plm_full_total))

#bind them together into a temporary data frame
vif_data <- cbind(transformed_Y, transformed_X)

#run standard lm() because R now sees them as completely separate columns
pure_vif_check <- lm(transformed_Y ~ ., data = vif_data)
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
vif(pure_vif_check)
# > vif(pure_vif_check)
# lag.yoy.AMECO.rev lag.yoy.AMECO.exp     lag.yoy.labor    lag.yoy.output 
# 1.389667          1.059717          1.544891          1.267598 
```

lag.yoy.AMECO.rev and lag.yoy.labor increased slightly whereas the other
two decreased slightly.

I will also recheck normality since Ireland introduces at least one outlier (2015).

```{r echo=FALSE}
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
resids <- as.numeric(residuals(H2_plm_full_total))

qqnorm(resids, main = "H2 Total Unemployment incl. Ireland - QQ Plot of Panel Residuals",
       ylab='standardized residuals')
qqline(resids, col = "red")
```

Similar to our QQ plot in H1, the scale has increased but otherwise the plot is
more of less the same. Our plot still follow the theoretical quantiles from -1.5 
to 1.5, but now extends past 3 on the standardized residual y-axis.
Overall normality is still met.

## H2 INCLUDING IRELAND LEVERAGE AND COOK'S DISTANCE

```{r include=FALSE}
#reset row names to sequence from 1 to the total number of rows
rownames(H2_final) <- NULL
#use least squares dummy variable 
H2_lsdv <- lm(unemployment.total.yoy ~ lag.yoy.AMECO.rev + lag.yoy.AMECO.exp +
                lag.yoy.output + lag.yoy.labor + 
                factor(geo) + factor(TIME_PERIOD), data = H2_final)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
#Residuals vs Leverage plot
plot(H2_lsdv, which = 5, main = "H2 Total Unemployment incl. Ireland - Residuals vs Leverage")

#Cook's Distance by Observation Index
cooks_vals <- cooks.distance(H2_lsdv)
plot(cooks_vals, type = "h", main = "Cook's Distance", ylab = "Cook's D Value")

# Add a horizontal line for the 4/n threshold
abline(h = 4 / nrow(H2_final), col = "red", lty = 2)

#find the country and year for our top leverage points
H2_final[c(177, 116, 94), c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD")]
```

Observation 177 (Spain, 2009): This data point represents a high residual, high
leverage outlier driven by an unexpected macroeconomic shock. Spain sits high on
the vertical axis with a standardized residual approaching +4. This indicates that
the spike in Spain's national unemployment rate in 2009 was roughly four standard deviations
higher than what the model’s predictors (lagged policy mix, output gap changes,
and productivity shifts) anticipated. The domestic collapse of the Spanish
construction sector and credit markets triggered a structural shock to employment
that far outpaced the baseline Eurozone trend.

Observation 116 (Ireland, 2016): This data point represent high leverage and moderate
residual, exhibiting extreme leverage on the horizontal axis (leverage > 0.55).
Because the independent policy variables are scaled relative to potential GDP,
the massive 2015 Irish corporate accounting distortion severely warped the baseline
denominators for the subsequent year's metrics. While its residual is moderate (around -2),
its extreme horizontal positioning forces it to exert significant pulling force on
the overall regression slope.

Observation 94 (Greece, 2011): This observation presents a combination of high
leverage (approx 0.28) and a high positive residual (approx +2.7). 2011 represents
the peak implementation phase of Greece's structural adjustment programs.
The model significantly underpredicted the scale of public and private sector job
losses, making this a pivotal observation for evaluating the actual human cost of
the austerity mix.

The Cook's Distance plot highlights a sample-size adjusted threshold at 4/n
(the red dashed line, approx 0.02). In contrast to the GDP model (where Ireland
2015 sat alone as a solitary tower), the labor market model shows two distinct
structural skyscrapers reaching Cook's D values above 0.20
Index 116 (Ireland, 2016): Driven entirely by extreme leverage inherited from
the 2015 corporate accounting shift.Index 177 (Spain, 2009): Driven by the sheer
scale of the unpredicted surge in unemployment following the burst housing and
banking bubble. A localized cluster of volatility is also clearly visible between
indices 90 and 120, mapping the continuous structural disruptions across the
European Periphery (Greece and Ireland) during the height of the consolidation years.

The Case for Keeping Spain 2009 and Greece 2011: These points represent authentic,
real-world labor market crises. Removing them would defeat the purpose of the thesis.
Their high Cook's Distance is "informative leverage" because they provide the exact
data variation needed to determine if spending cuts (beta_exp) or tax hikes (beta_rev)
cause a worse spike in unemployment.

The Case for Ireland 2016: This point is a mathematical distortion. Because it sits
far to the right with extreme leverage, it is actively anchoring the regression line.
This strongly justifies running our planned sensitivity analysis completely excluding
Ireland for H2 as well. Dropping Ireland will remove that artificial right-hand anchor,
allowing the model to cleanly isolate how economic momentum (the output gap) and the
austerity mix truly dictate employment shifts across regular Eurozone labor markets.

## HYPOTHESIS 2 TOTAL UNEMPLOYMENT INCLUDING IRELAND ANALYSIS

```{r include=FALSE}
#apply Driscoll-Kraay Standard Errors
H2_final_results <- coeftest(H2_plm_full_total, 
                          vcov = vcovSCC(H2_plm_full_total, type = "HC3", cluster = "time"))
```

Null Hypothesis 1: Holding the total size of fiscal consolidation constant (budget deficit 
reduction of the exact same size), the impact of cyclically adjusted expenditure
cuts on the Real GDP growth rate is equal to the impact of cyclically adjusted 
revenue increases. (The specific mix of austerity does not matter) (beta_exp = beta_rev).

Alternative Hypothesis 1: The impact of cyclically adjusted expenditure cuts on 
the Real GDP growth rate is significantly different from the impact of cyclically 
adjusted revenue increases. (The specific mix of austerity alters the severity of
the economic slowdown) (beta_exp != beta_rev).

```{r echo=FALSE}
print(H2_final_results)
# > print(H2_final_results)
# 
# t test of coefficients:
#   
#   Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
# lag.yoy.AMECO.rev -0.177909   0.217304 -0.8187  0.41420    
# lag.yoy.AMECO.exp  0.091972   0.035741  2.5733  0.01101 *  
# lag.yoy.labor      0.061234   0.056942  1.0754  0.28386    
# lag.yoy.output    -0.585032   0.095172 -6.1471 6.33e-09 ***
#   ---
#   Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1
```

Compared to our previous results that excluded Ireland, the cyclically adjusted
expenditure variable (lag.yoy.AMECO.exp) actually experiences an increase in
both its coefficient size and statistical significance, moving from an estimate
of 0.0658 (p = 0.038) up to 0.0920 (p = 0.011). Meanwhile, our control variables
are heavily dampened; the lagged labor productivity growth variable (lag.yoy.labor)
completely loses its significance, dropping from a highly significant p = 0.008
down to an insignificant p = 0.284, and the output gap momentum control is noticeably
weakened. Because Ireland's massive 2015 corporate accounting restructurings
injected an artificial, non-policy-driven surge into the potential GDP denominator
used to scale our primary fiscal predictors, keeping Ireland in the dataset introduces
unique cross-sectional distortions.In this specific labor framework, the inclusion
of Ireland forces an artificial re-weighting of the panel's error variance.
By coupling highly volatile, corporate-skewed fiscal ratios with Ireland's genuine,
steep post-crisis unemployment recovery, the model yields an inflated expenditure
coefficient and disrupted control slopes. This structural interference masks the true
labor market dynamics common to typical Eurozone economies by introducing severe
denominator-driven noise into the tracking of domestic policy shocks. Therefore,
running this secondary check successfully demonstrates that while expenditure cuts
appear even more contractionary on paper when Ireland is included, dropping Ireland
remains a necessary step to protect the panel's underlying parameters from localized
macroeconomic anomalies and capture the true, unskewed relationship between structural
fiscal choices and national unemployment trajectories.

Formal Wald Test 

```{r echo=FALSE}
linearHypothesis(H2_plm_full_total, 
                 "lag.yoy.AMECO.rev = lag.yoy.AMECO.exp", 
                 vcov = vcovSCC(H2_plm_full_total, type = "HC3", cluster = "time"))

# > linearHypothesis(H2_plm_full_total, 
#                    +                  "lag.yoy.AMECO.rev = lag.yoy.AMECO.exp", 
#                    +                  vcov = vcovSCC(H2_plm_full_total, type = "HC3", cluster = "time"))
# 
# Linear hypothesis test:
#   lag.yoy.AMECO.rev - lag.yoy.AMECO.exp = 0
# 
# Model 1: restricted model
# Model 2: unemployment.total.yoy ~ lag.yoy.AMECO.rev + lag.yoy.AMECO.exp + 
#   lag.yoy.labor + lag.yoy.output
# 
# Note: Coefficient covariance matrix supplied.
# 
# Res.Df Df  Chisq Pr(>Chisq)
# 1    157                     
# 2    156  1 1.2903      0.256
```

A final formal Wald test was run on the full dataset including Ireland to see if
the impact of revenue changes is statistically different from expenditure changes
(beta_lag.yoy.AMECO.rev = beta_lag.yoy.AMECO.exp). Employing the same Driscoll-Kraay
robust covariance matrix with HC3 corrections, the test yields a Chi-squared statistic
of 1.2903 with a p-value of 0.256. Because this is well above our 0.05 significance
threshold, we fail to reject Null Hypothesis 2.When Ireland is included, the
statistical gap between the policy levers becomes even more attenuated, as the
p-value slips from 0.1439 further up to a completely insignificant 0.256.
This happens because Ireland's massive, unrelated 2015 corporate accounting surge
distorts the potential GDP denominators used to scale our primary policy predictors.
This asset-shifting anomaly introduces structural variance that broadens the
covariance boundaries and blurs the underlying policy differences we isolated in
the primary model. This outcome further demonstrates that keeping Ireland in the
dataset introduces severe measurement distortions that mask how these distinct
fiscal levers actually behave across typical Eurozone labor markets.

## HYPOTHESIS 2 YOUTH UNEMPLOYMENT EX IRELAND ASSUMPTION CHECKS

Now I will re-run a model that uses the same predictors but instead uses a dependent
variable for youth unemployment YoY changes. The previous model was for total
unemployment, and I would like to analyze if there is any difference in how
fiscal austerity measures impact the two unemployment rates.

```{r echo=TRUE}
H2_plm_no_ireland_youth <- plm(unemployment.youth.yoy ~ lag.yoy.AMECO.rev + lag.yoy.AMECO.exp + 
                                 lag.yoy.labor + lag.yoy.output,
                               data = H2_final_no_ireland,
                               index = c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD"),
                               model = "within", #within = fixed effects
                               effect = "twoways")
```

```{r include=FALSE}
#extract the within-transformed (demeaned) variables properly
transformed_X <- as.data.frame(model.matrix(H2_plm_no_ireland_youth))
transformed_Y <- as.numeric(pmodel.response(H2_plm_no_ireland_youth))

#bind them together into a temporary data frame
vif_data <- cbind(transformed_Y, transformed_X)

#run standard lm() because R now sees them as completely separate columns
pure_vif_check <- lm(transformed_Y ~ ., data = vif_data)
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
vif(pure_vif_check)
# > vif(pure_vif_check)
# lag.yoy.AMECO.rev lag.yoy.AMECO.exp     lag.yoy.labor    lag.yoy.output 
# 1.203651          1.126422          1.411148          1.443360 
```

All predictors have the same VIF values as they did in the total unemployment
model, as expected since only the dependent variable changed

### LINEARITY CHECK

```{r include=FALSE}
#extract the clean, model-transformed dependent variable (excludes dropped rows)
actual_Y <- as.numeric(pmodel.response(H2_plm_no_ireland_youth))
#extract the model residuals
resids <- as.numeric(residuals(H2_plm_no_ireland_youth))
#calculate fitted values using matching dimensions
fitted_values <- actual_Y - resids

par(mfrow = c(1,1))
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
plot(fitted_values, resids, 
     main = "H2 Youth Unemployment ex Ireland - Residuals vs Fitted", 
     xlab = "Fitted Values", ylab = "Residuals",
     pch = 19, col = rgb(0, 0, 0, 0.5))
abline(h = 0, col = "red", lwd = 2)

#LOESS smooth trend line (Blue, solid
lines(lowess(fitted_values, resids, f=0.75), col = "blue", lwd = 2)
```

All residuals are evenly scattered above and below 0. Our blue LOESS trend line is 
straight with no curve and stays close to 0 within the main cloud of points. 
There is no shape or pattern in the residuals and they appear randomly scattered.
The blue LOESS trend line does spike up at the right side from the high residual,
large fitted values, but this is because there are not other points in that area
to influence it. Since the trend line is straight with no pattern and centered 
near 0, I will conclude that linearity is met.
As for homoscedasticity, there are some residuals that have much higher variance,
relative to the main scatter of points, so homoscedasticity may not be met.
I will run a formal Breusch-Pagan test to check homoscedasticity and apply 
Driscoll-Kraay standard errors regardless of the results to further protect
my model's standard errors, just like the previous hypotheses.
As for the spread on the x-axis, there are some outlier fitted values on the right 
and left, but it is only a handful of values. I will create a residual vs leverage
plot to make sure these points are not strongly influencing my regression model.
Overall, I will say linearity is met.

### INDEPENDENCE CHECK

Test for Autocorrelation within countries
H0: errors are independent (no autocorrelation)
H1: errors are not independent (autocorrelation exists)

```{r echo=FALSE}
pbgtest(H2_plm_no_ireland_youth) #Breusch-Godfrey test for panel data
# > pbgtest(H2_plm_no_ireland_youth) #Breusch-Godfrey test for panel data
# 
# Breusch-Godfrey/Wooldridge test for serial correlation in panel models
# 
# data:  unemployment.youth.yoy ~ lag.yoy.AMECO.rev + lag.yoy.AMECO.exp +  ...
# chisq = 25.746, df = 17, p-value = 0.07921
# alternative hypothesis: serial correlation in idiosyncratic errors
```

With a significance level of 0.05 and a p-value of 0.07921, we fail to reject
H0 and conclude that our errors are independent and do not suffer from autocorrelation

Test for Cross-Sectional Dependence (Spatial correlation between countries)
H0: errors are independent
H1: errors are not independent

```{r echo=FALSE}
pcdtest(H2_plm_no_ireland_youth, test = "lm")
# > pcdtest(H2_plm_no_ireland_youth, test = "lm")
# 
# Breusch-Pagan LM test for cross-sectional dependence in panels
# 
# data:  unemployment.youth.yoy ~ lag.yoy.AMECO.rev + lag.yoy.AMECO.exp + lag.yoy.labor + lag.yoy.output
# chisq = 75.796, df = 45, p-value = 0.002759
# alternative hypothesis: cross-sectional dependence
```

With a significance level of 0.05 and a p-value of 0.002759, we reject H0 and 
conclude that errors are not independent between countries

Since autocorrelation is present between countries,we apply Driscoll-Kraay 
standard errors for the same reasons used in H1

### NORMALITY CHECK

```{r echo=FALSE}
#extract clean numeric residuals
resids <- as.numeric(residuals(H2_plm_no_ireland_youth))

qqnorm(resids, main = "H2 Youth Unemployment ex Ireland - QQ Plot of Panel Residuals",
       ylab='standardized residuals')
qqline(resids, col = "red")
```

Our QQ plot is worse compared to total unemployment, with the scale jumping
up to +/-6 in order to fit the increased variability of youth unemployment compared
to aggregate unemployment. However, our plot follows the theoretical quantiles 
from -1.5 to 1.5 pretty well, with larger tails compared to aggregate unemployment.
Furthermore, with 187 observations, (198-11), the CLT ensures that because our 
sample size is large, we are guaranteed that the distribution of our coefficient
estimates will form a perfect normal distribution, allowing us to trust our final
results, regardless of the distributions of our raw data.
Overall, while the strict assumption of residual normality is technically violated
in the tails due to predictable crisis-driven outliers, the asymptotic properties 
of our sample size render the model highly robust and statistically valid.

### EQUAL VARIANCE CHECK

H0: errors have equal variance
H1: errors do not have equal variance

```{r echo=FALSE}
bptest(H2_plm_no_ireland_youth)
# > bptest(H2_plm_no_ireland_youth)
# 
# studentized Breusch-Pagan test
# 
# data:  H2_plm_no_ireland_youth
# BP = 21.748, df = 4, p-value = 0.0002249
```

Since out p-value is 0.0002, this is less than our significance level of 0.05,
so we reject H0 and conclude that our errors do not have equal variance. This is
expected because out 11 countries are not all uniform. A 1% increase in fiscal
tightening can cause predictable responses in one country but highly volatile
responses in another. Furthermore, the impact of the crisis was highly unequal among
the included nations, with the peripheral countries ultimately receiving the most economic
impact relative to the core Eurozone countries.

Because the data has already been transformed via a Two-Way Within specification
to resolve fixed effect dummy bloat, we can apply non-parametric covariance
adjustments directly to the active variations. We implement Driscoll-Kraay (SCC)
standard errors. This framework is uniquely suited to address the Breusch-Pagan
violation because it modifies the variance-covariance matrix to be entirely robust
to heteroscedasticity. Furthermore, it simultaneously corrects for spatial
cross-sectional dependence (the tendency of Eurozone labor markets to move together
due to shared monetary policy) and temporal autocorrelation up to a designated
lag length (t-2). By mapping the Driscoll-Kraay correction onto the demeaning
framework, the uneven variance identified by the Breusch-Pagan test is entirely
absorbed. This ensures that the final significance testing and confidence intervals
for our expenditure and revenue predictors are statistically conservative.

### H2 YOUTH UNEMPLOYMENT EXLUDING IRELAND COOK'S DISTANCE AND LEVERAGE CHECK

```{r include=FALSE}
#reset row names to sequence from 1 to the total number of rows
rownames(H2_final_no_ireland) <- NULL
#use least squares dummy variable 
H2_lsdv <- lm(unemployment.youth.yoy ~ lag.yoy.AMECO.rev + lag.yoy.AMECO.exp +
                lag.yoy.output + lag.yoy.labor + 
                factor(geo) + factor(TIME_PERIOD), data = H2_final_no_ireland)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
#Residuals vs Leverage plot
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
plot(H2_lsdv, which = 5, main = "H2 Youth Unemployment ex Ireland - Residuals vs Leverage")

#Cook's Distance by Observation Index
cooks_vals <- cooks.distance(H2_lsdv)
plot(cooks_vals, type = "h", main = "Cook's Distance", ylab = "Cook's D Value")

#Add a horizontal line for the 4/n threshold
abline(h = 4 / nrow(H2_final_no_ireland), col = "red", lty = 2)

#find the country and year for our top leverage points
H2_final_no_ireland[c(93, 145, 160), c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD")]
# > H2_final_no_ireland[c(93, 145, 160), c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD")]
# geo TIME_PERIOD
# 93    Greece        2010
# 145 Portugal        2011
# 160    Spain        2009
```

The diagnostics highlight three critical observations—Portugal 2011, Greece 2010,
and Spain 2009—that exert substantial influence over the model's structure.
Observation 160 (Spain, 2009): is a prominent high-leverage, high-residual outlier,
displaying a large positive standardized residual of approximately +3.0 and leverage
approaching 0.45. This point captures the severe and immediate youth labor market
crisis triggered by the collapse of Spain's construction sector and housing bubble,
which disproportionately devastated entry-level and young workers.

Observation 145 (Portugal, 2011): represents a large positive-residual outlier
with a standardized residual of approximately +3.4 and moderate leverage around 0.18,
reflecting the shock to youth employment during the year Portugal requested its
EU-IMF bailout and implemented deep public sector spending freezes.

Observation 93 (Greece, 2010): exhibits a substantial positive standardized residual
of approximately +3.3 and moderate leverage near 0.19, capturing the early phase
of the Greek debt crisis where young workers bore the initial brunt of collapsing
domestic demand.

On the Cook's Distance plot, while the vast majority of the 11-country cohort remains
stable and well-behaved below the sample-size adjusted heuristic limit of 4/n
(approximately 0.02), these three peripheral observations form a clear cluster
of systemic volatility. Observation 160 (Spain, 2009) registers the absolute highest
Cook's D value in the sample, peaking at approximately 0.18, while Observations
93 (Greece, 2010) and 145 (Portugal, 2011) follow closely with Cook's D values of
approximately 0.08 and 0.09, respectively. Although these points do not pass the
conservative 1.0 threshold on the Residuals vs. Leverage plot, they exert a
disproportionate influence over the final beta estimates, serving as the primary
drivers of volatility in the youth unemployment model.

Despite their high leverage and substantial Cook's Distance values, these observations
are intentionally retained in the baseline model. Youth labor markets are highly
sensitive, often reacting non-linearly to macroeconomic contractions, making the
inclusion of these severe crisis episodes vital. The spikes in youth unemployment
across Spain, Greece, and Portugal represent genuine macroeconomic realities. Removing
these countries during their most volatile periods would introduce severe selection
bias, strip the model of the actual labor market shocks the study aims to analyze,
artificially smooth the panel, and ultimately yield a model that fails to capture
the catastrophic real-world impact of fiscal consolidation on the Eurozone's younger
workforce.

## HYPOTHESIS 2 YOUTH UNEMPLOYMENT EXCLUDING IRELAND ANALYSIS

```{r include=FALSE}
#apply Driscoll-Kraay Standard Errors to H2 plm model
H2_final_results <- coeftest(H2_plm_no_ireland_youth, 
                             vcov = vcovSCC(H2_plm_no_ireland_youth, 
                                            type = "HC3", 
                                            cluster = "time"))
```

Null Hypothesis 2: Holding the total size of fiscal consolidation constant (budget deficit 
reduction of the exact same size), the impact of cyclically adjusted expenditure
cuts on the national unemployment rate is equal to the impact of cyclically adjusted
revenue increases. (The specific mix of austerity has no unique relationship with
job losses) (beta_exp = beta_rev).

Alternative Hypothesis 2: The impact of cyclically adjusted expenditure cuts on 
the national unemployment rate is significantly different from the impact of 
cyclically adjusted revenue increases. (Shifting the balance toward spending cuts
changes how many jobs are lost during consolidation) (beta_exp != beta_rev).

```{r echo=FALSE}
print(H2_final_results)
# > print(H2_final_results)
# 
# t test of coefficients:
#   
#                     Estimate Std. Error t value  Pr(>|t|)    
# lag.yoy.AMECO.rev -0.413664   0.439996 -0.9402 0.3487585    
# lag.yoy.AMECO.exp -0.023531   0.075773 -0.3105 0.7566049    
# lag.yoy.labor      0.424937   0.112748  3.7689 0.0002409 ***
#   lag.yoy.output  -1.444322   0.149909 -9.6347 < 2.2e-16 ***
#   ---
#   Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1
```

Lagged Cyclically Adjusted Revenues (lag.yoy.AMECO.rev):
The Coefficient is -0.4137 with a p-value of p = 0.349. Holding all else constant,
a 1 percentage point increase in the lagged YoY change of cyclically adjusted
revenues (revenue-based consolidation/tax hikes) is associated with a 0.414
percentage point decrease in the youth unemployment rate change the following year.
The high p-value is well above our significance level of 0.05, so we fail to reject
H0 and conclude that revenue-based policy changes in year t-1 have a statistically
insignificant relationship with the youth unemployment rate trajectory in the
following year t. From a policy standpoint, increasing or decreasing structural
revenues via tax hikes or cuts does not reliably push youth unemployment growth
deviations up or down in the following year.

Lagged Cyclically Adjusted Expenditures (lag.yoy.AMECO.exp):
The Coefficient is -0.0235 with a p-value of p = 0.757. Holding all else constant,
a 1 percentage point increase in the lagged YoY change of cyclically adjusted
expenditures is associated with a 0.024 percentage point decrease in the youth
unemployment rate change the following year. The p-value is well above our significance
level of 0.05, so we fail to reject H0 and conclude that expenditure-based changes
in year t-1 do not have a statistically significant individual impact on the change
in the youth unemployment rate in the following year t. Unlike the aggregate model
where spending cuts significantly accelerated job losses, the youth labor market
coefficient drops close to zero and flips negative, demonstrating that individual
spending adjustments fail to show a structurally reliable or statistically significant
direct link to immediate youth employment deviations within this sample.

Lagged Labor Productivity Growth (lag.yoy.labor):
The Coefficient is +0.4249 with a p-value of p < 0.001. Holding all else constant,
a 1 percentage point increase in the lagged YoY change of real labor productivity
per hour worked is associated with a 0.425 percentage point increase in the youth
unemployment rate change in year t. The purpose of this coefficient is to establish
the structural health and technological resilience of a country's workforce prior
to fiscal adjustments, ensuring our austerity coefficients are not biased by pre-existing
labor decay. Because it is extraordinarily statistically significant (p < 0.001),
it highlights that accelerating workforce productivity acts as a severe independent
driver of upward shocks in youth unemployment, capturing intense structural labor-shedding
and "last-in, first-out" hiring freezes that disproportionately penalize younger entrants.

Lagged Change in Output Gap (lag.yoy.output):
The Coefficient is -1.4443 with a p-value of p < 0.001. Holding all else constant,
a 1 percentage point increase in the lagged change of the output gap is associated
with a 1.444 percentage point decrease in the youth unemployment rate change in
the following year t. The purpose of this variable is to control for the cyclical
position and momentum of the economy entering the fiscal period. Mirroring macroeconomic
expectations but showing twice the absolute sensitivity seen in the aggregate model,
this relationship is highly statistically significant (p < 0.001), proving that
overall economic momentum and cyclical expansions exert a massive, dominant role
in decelerating youth unemployment pressures within this two-way demeaning frame.

Formal test whether spending cuts and tax hikes have equal impacts on unemployment.

```{r include=FALSE}
H2_hypothesis_test <- linearHypothesis(H2_plm_no_ireland_youth, 
                                       "lag.yoy.AMECO.rev = lag.yoy.AMECO.exp", 
                                       vcov = vcovSCC(H2_plm_no_ireland_youth, 
                                                      type = "HC3", 
                                                      cluster = "time"))
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
print(H2_hypothesis_test)
# > print(H2_hypothesis_test)
# 
# Linear hypothesis test:
#   lag.yoy.AMECO.rev - lag.yoy.AMECO.exp = 0
# 
# Model 1: restricted model
# Model 2: unemployment.youth.yoy ~ lag.yoy.AMECO.rev + lag.yoy.AMECO.exp + 
#   lag.yoy.labor + lag.yoy.output
# 
# Note: Coefficient covariance matrix supplied.
# 
# Res.Df Df  Chisq Pr(>Chisq)
# 1    141                     
# 2    140  1 0.6403     0.4236
```

OVERALL HYPOTHESIS 2 YOUTH UNEMPLOYMENT EX IRELAND CONCLUSION:
While the individual coefficient testing showed that both lagged cyclical
revenue-based adjustments (p = 0.349) and expenditure-based adjustments (p = 0.757)
were individually statistical noise, a formal Linear Restriction Test (Wald Test)
was run to directly test the hypothesis lag.yoy.AMECO.rev - lag.yoy.AMECO.exp = 0.
Utilizing a Driscoll-Kraay robust covariance matrix with HC3 corrections to
adjust for spatial and cross-sectional dependence, the test yields a Chi-squared
statistic of 0.6403 with a p-value of 0.4236. Because this p-value is well above
our 0.05 significance threshold, we fail to reject Null Hypothesis 2.
Therefore, we cannot conclude that the macroeconomic impact on the youth unemployment
rate of an expenditure cut is significantly different from the impact of a revenue
increase of the exact same size. The standard error for lag.yoy.AMECO.rev was
exceptionally large (0.4400) relative to its estimate (-0.4137), and the standard
error for lag.yoy.AMECO.exp was 0.0758 relative to its near-zero estimate
(-0.0235). Because the youth labor market introduces immense structural volatility,
the model is highly uncertain about the true underlying parameters of these fiscal
levers, resulting in wide confidence intervals that overlap completely.
Furthermore, slicing a panel model down by removing Ireland and applying two-way
fixed effects strips out a massive amount of variation and helps eliminate bias.
However, with N=10 countries and a relatively short time series, the extreme cyclical
variance inherent to youth employment demands a high threshold of structural
divergence to declare two policy coefficients statistically different from one
another, hence we fail to reject H0.

## HYPOTHESIS 2 YOUTH UNEMPLOYMENT INCLUDING IRELAND ANALYSIS

Now we will re-run the model with Ireland included in the dataset for the 
sane reasons discussed for the total unemployment model.

```{r echo=TRUE}
H2_plm_full_youth <- plm(unemployment.youth.yoy ~ lag.yoy.AMECO.rev + lag.yoy.AMECO.exp + 
                           lag.yoy.labor + lag.yoy.output,
                         data = H2_final,
                         index = c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD"),
                         model = "within", #within = fixed effects
                         effect = "twoways")
```

### NORMAILTY CHECK

I will recheck normality since Ireland introduces at least one outlier (2015)
extract clean numeric residuals

```{r echo=FALSE}
resids <- as.numeric(residuals(H2_plm_full_youth))
par(mfrow = c(1,1))
qqnorm(resids, main = "H2 Youth Umeployment incl. Ireland - QQ Plot of Panel Residuals",
       ylab='standardized residuals')
qqline(resids, col = "red")
```

Our QQ plot looks very similar to our QQ plot excluding Ireland, except it does
not follow the theoretical quantiles are cleanly between +/- 1.5. I will conclude
that normality is met. The tails are about the same, with the same general shape
and scale of deviations.
Overall, while the strict assumption of residual normality is technically violated
in the tails due to predictable crisis-driven outliers, the asymptotic properties 
of our sample size render the model highly robust and statistically valid.

### H2 YOUTH UNEMPLOYMENT INCLUDING IRELAND COOK'S DISTANCE AND LEVERAGE CHECK

```{r include=FALSE}
#reset row names to sequence from 1 to the total number of rows
rownames(H2_final) <- NULL
#use least squares dummy variable 
H2_lsdv <- lm(unemployment.youth.yoy ~ lag.yoy.AMECO.rev + lag.yoy.AMECO.exp +
                lag.yoy.output + lag.yoy.labor + 
                factor(geo) + factor(TIME_PERIOD), data = H2_final)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
#Residuals vs Leverage plot
plot(H2_lsdv, which = 5, main = "H2 Youth Unemployment incl. Ireland - Residuals vs Leverage")

#Cook's Distance by Observation Index
cooks_vals <- cooks.distance(H2_lsdv)
plot(cooks_vals, type = "h", main = "Cook's Distance", ylab = "Cook's D Value")

# Add a horizontal line for the 4/n threshold
abline(h = 4 / nrow(H2_final), col = "red", lty = 2)

#find the country and year for our top leverage points
H2_final[c(177, 116, 94), c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD")]
```

Observation 177 (Spain, 2009): This data point represents a high residual, high
leverage outlier driven by an unexpected macroeconomic shock. Spain sits high on
the vertical axis with a standardized residual approaching +4. This indicates that
the spike in Spain's national unemployment rate in 2009 was roughly four standard deviations
higher than what the model’s predictors (lagged policy mix, output gap changes,
and productivity shifts) anticipated. The domestic collapse of the Spanish
construction sector and credit markets triggered a structural shock to employment
that far outpaced the baseline Eurozone trend.

Observation 116 (Ireland, 2016): This data point represent high leverage and moderate
residual, exhibiting extreme leverage on the horizontal axis (leverage > 0.55).
Because the independent policy variables are scaled relative to potential GDP,
the massive 2015 Irish corporate accounting distortion severely warped the baseline
denominators for the subsequent year's metrics. While its residual is moderate (around -2),
its extreme horizontal positioning forces it to exert significant pulling force on
the overall regression slope.

Observation 94 (Greece, 2011): This observation presents a combination of high
leverage (approx 0.28) and a high positive residual (approx +2.7). 2011 represents
the peak implementation phase of Greece's structural adjustment programs.
The model significantly underpredicted the scale of public and private sector job
losses, making this a pivotal observation for evaluating the actual human cost of
the austerity mix.

The Cook's Distance plot highlights a sample-size adjusted threshold at 4/n
(the red dashed line, approx 0.02). In contrast to the GDP model (where Ireland
2015 sat alone as a solitary tower), the labor market model shows two distinct
structural skyscrapers reaching Cook's D values above 0.20
Index 116 (Ireland, 2016): Driven entirely by extreme leverage inherited from
the 2015 corporate accounting shift.Index 177 (Spain, 2009): Driven by the sheer
scale of the unpredicted surge in unemployment following the burst housing and
banking bubble. A localized cluster of volatility is also clearly visible between
indices 90 and 120, mapping the continuous structural disruptions across the
European Periphery (Greece and Ireland) during the height of the consolidation years.

The Case for Keeping Spain 2009 and Greece 2011: These points represent authentic,
real-world labor market crises. Removing them would defeat the purpose of the thesis.
Their high Cook's Distance is "informative leverage" because they provide the exact
data variation needed to determine if spending cuts (beta_exp) or tax hikes (beta_rev)
cause a worse spike in unemployment.

The Case for Ireland 2016: This point is a mathematical distortion. Because it sits
far to the right with extreme leverage, it is actively anchoring the regression line.
This strongly justifies running our planned sensitivity analysis completely excluding
Ireland for H2 as well. Dropping Ireland will remove that artificial right-hand anchor,
allowing the model to cleanly isolate how economic momentum (the output gap) and the
austerity mix truly dictate employment shifts across regular Eurozone labor markets.

## HYPOTHESIS 2 YOUTH UNEMPLOYMENT INCLUDING IRELAND ANALYSIS

```{r include=FALSE}
#apply Driscoll-Kraay Standard Errors
H2_final_results <- coeftest(H2_plm_full_youth, 
                             vcov = vcovSCC(H2_plm_full_youth, type = "HC3", cluster = "time"))
```

Null Hypothesis 2: Holding the total size of fiscal consolidation constant (budget deficit 
reduction of the exact same size), the impact of cyclically adjusted expenditure
cuts on the Real GDP growth rate is equal to the impact of cyclically adjusted 
revenue increases. (The specific mix of austerity does not matter) (beta_exp = beta_rev).

Alternative Hypothesis 2: The impact of cyclically adjusted expenditure cuts on 
the Real GDP growth rate is significantly different from the impact of cyclically 
adjusted revenue increases. (The specific mix of austerity alters the severity of
the economic slowdown) (beta_exp != beta_rev).

```{r echo=FALSE}
print(H2_final_results)
# > print(H2_final_results)
# 
# t test of coefficients:
#   
#   Estimate Std. Error t value  Pr(>|t|)    
# lag.yoy.AMECO.rev -0.250407   0.440833 -0.5680   0.57083    
# lag.yoy.AMECO.exp  0.102527   0.058097  1.7647   0.07956 .  
# lag.yoy.labor      0.158074   0.145643  1.0853   0.27944    
# lag.yoy.output    -1.121193   0.186044 -6.0265 1.163e-08 ***
#   ---
#   Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1
```

Compared to our previous youth model results that excluded Ireland, the inclusion
of the full dataset causes the core fiscal policy coefficients to shift and
remain statistically insignificant at the 5% level, though the cyclically
adjusted expenditure variable (lag.yoy.AMECO.exp) is now marginally significant
at the 10% level (p = 0.080). The revenue coefficient shifts from -0.4137 to
-0.2504 (p = 0.571), while the expenditure coefficient moves from a near-zero
estimate of -0.0235 up to 0.1025. Concurrently, the lagged labor productivity
control variable (lag.yoy.labor) completely loses its strong individual
significance, dropping from a highly significant p < 0.001 down to an insignificant
p = 0.279, and the output gap momentum control is noticeably dampened.
Because Ireland’s multinational-driven 2015 corporate accounting spike injected
a massive, non-policy-driven surge into the potential GDP denominator used to
scale our primary fiscal predictors, keeping Ireland in the dataset introduces
severe cross-sectional distortions. In this youth framework, matching Ireland's
volatile, asset-inflated fiscal ratios with its steep post-crisis youth employment
recovery forces an artificial distortion of the panel's underlying parameters.
This denominator-driven noise heavy-handedly disrupts the control slopes and
shifts the expenditure parameter, masking the true structural dynamics common
to typical Eurozone youth labor markets. Therefore, running this secondary check
conclusively demonstrates that dropping Ireland remains a necessary step to
protect the panel's error variance and capture the true, unskewed relationship
between structural fiscal levers and youth unemployment trajectories.

Formal Wald Test

```{r echo=FALSE}
linearHypothesis(H2_plm_full_youth, 
                 "lag.yoy.AMECO.rev = lag.yoy.AMECO.exp", 
                 vcov = vcovSCC(H2_plm_full_youth, type = "HC3", cluster = "time"))

# > linearHypothesis(H2_plm_full_youth, 
#                    +                  "lag.yoy.AMECO.rev = lag.yoy.AMECO.exp", 
#                    +                  vcov = vcovSCC(H2_plm_full_youth, type = "HC3", cluster = "time"))
# 
# Linear hypothesis test:
#   lag.yoy.AMECO.rev - lag.yoy.AMECO.exp = 0
# 
# Model 1: restricted model
# Model 2: unemployment.youth.yoy ~ lag.yoy.AMECO.rev + lag.yoy.AMECO.exp + 
#   lag.yoy.labor + lag.yoy.output
# 
# Note: Coefficient covariance matrix supplied.
# 
# Res.Df Df  Chisq Pr(>Chisq)
# 1    157                     
# 2    156  1 0.5502     0.4583
```

A final formal Wald test was run on the full youth dataset including Ireland to
see if the impact of revenue changes is statistically different from expenditure
changes (beta_lag.yoy.AMECO.rev = beta_lag.yoy.AMECO.exp).
Employing the same Driscoll-Kraay robust covariance matrix with HC3 corrections,
the test yields a Chi-squared statistic of 0.5502 with a p-value of 0.4583.
Because this is well above our 0.05 significance threshold, we fail to reject
Null Hypothesis 2.When Ireland is included, the statistical gap between the
policy levers moves even further into complete insignificance, with the p-value
climbing from 0.4236 in the clean model to 0.4583 in the full model. This occurs
because Ireland's distorted globalization metrics alter the expenditure slope
and inflate the standard errors, blurring the descriptive policy differences
isolated in our primary model. This outcome mirrors our findings from the total
unemployment model, providing verification that including Ireland introduces
structural noise that actively hides the true macroeconomic transmission channels
of austerity policies in typical Eurozone economies.

<div style="page-break-after: always;"></div>

# HYPOTHESIS 3 ABSTRACT AND METHODOLOGY

Null Hypothesis 3: Holding total household disposable income, household wealth,
and credit conditions constant, the specific mix of fiscal consolidation
(Direct vs. Indirect taxation) has no differential impact on household consumption
growth (beta_indirect = beta_direct)

Alternative Hypothesis 3: For an equivalent reduction in purchasing power, shifting
the tax burden toward indirect taxation (VAT) causes a significantly sharper
contraction in household consumption growth than direct taxation
(beta_indirect != beta_direct), because consumption taxes are highly salient at
the point of sale and disproportionately target households with a higher marginal
propensity to consume (MPC).

This section shifts the analysis to the micro-foundations of aggregate demand,
testing whether the specific tax instruments chosen during fiscal consolidation
uniquely depress domestic consumption. Specifically, it tests the hypothesis that
shifting the revenue-generation burden toward indirect taxation (VAT) causes a
significantly sharper contraction in real household consumption growth than direct
income taxes due to high salience and heightened exposure among 
high-marginal-propensity-to-consume (MPC) households. The econometric framework
isolates tax adjustments from primary income dynamics, inflation shocks, household
wealth destruction (housing asset collapses), and credit market rationing.
To prevent structural economic mismeasurement and artificial influence on the model
as seen in the two previous hypotheses, Ireland is permanently excluded
from this model and the overall sample for this hypothesis.

Dependent Variable Household Consumption Growth Rate:
Measured as the annual percentage change of final consumption expenditures
of households in chain-linked volumes. This isolates the real-time behavioral 
adjustment of consumers.

Predictor YoY change in direct tax revenues:
Measured as a percentage of nominal GDP. This captures the structural withdrawal 
of purchasing power via greater taxation on common forms of income.

Predictor YoY change in indirect tax revenues:
Measured as a percentage of nominal GDP. Captures increases in price via imports
and other indirect taxation methods. Utilizing these independent variables allows
a direct comparison of beta_indirect = beta_direct.

Predictor YoY change in gross primary income:
Measured in current prices. This controls for shocks to raw, pre-tax household 
spending power.

Predictor YoY change of the all-items Harmonized Index of Consumer Prices (HICP)
This holds country-specific real purchasing power fluctuations constant.

Predictor YoY change in consolidated private sector debt:
Measured as a percentage of GDP. This controls for supply-side banking credit crunches
and precautionary savings behavior.

Predictor YoY percentage change of the real residential property price index converted 
from quarterly averages to annual format. This isolates the catastrophic household
wealth-destruction shocks unique to the Spanish housing crashes.

```{r include=FALSE}
#_______________________________________________________________________________

#H3 IMPORT DATA AND CLEAN

#_______________________________________________________________________________


H3_tax <- read.csv(here('data', 'H3_direct_indirect_tax.csv'))
H3_primary_income <- read.csv(here('data', 'H3_gross_primary_income.csv'))
H3_HICP_inflation <- read.csv(here('data', 'H3_HICP_inflation.csv'))
H3_consumption <- read.csv(here('data', 'H3_household_consumption_growth_rate.csv'))
H3_credit <- read.csv(here('data', 'H3_domestic_credit.csv'))

readLines(here('data', 'H3_residential_real_property_prices.csv'), n = 15)
#contains metadata and was causing errors so must see which line data begins
#and import from there
H3_property_prices <- read.csv(here('data', 'H3_residential_real_property_prices.csv'), skip=3)

#remove unnecessary columns for all eurostat datasets and rearrange 
cols_remove_eurostat <- c('DATAFLOW', 'LAST.UPDATE', 'OBS_FLAG', 'CONF_STATUS', 'freq')

H3_tax[cols_remove_eurostat] <- list(NULL)
H3_HICP_inflation[cols_remove_eurostat] <- list(NULL)
H3_primary_income[cols_remove_eurostat] <- list(NULL)
H3_consumption[cols_remove_eurostat] <- list(NULL)
H3_credit[cols_remove_eurostat] <- list(NULL)

#rearrange columns so that main columns of interest are listed first
front_cols <- c('geo', 'TIME_PERIOD', 'unit', 'OBS_VALUE')
#find all the remaining columns
other_cols <- setdiff(names(H3_tax), front_cols)
H3_tax <- H3_tax[, c(front_cols, other_cols)]

other_cols <- setdiff(names(H3_HICP_inflation), front_cols)
H3_HICP_inflation <- H3_HICP_inflation[, c(front_cols, other_cols)]

other_cols <- setdiff(names(H3_primary_income), front_cols)
H3_primary_income <- H3_primary_income[, c(front_cols, other_cols)]

other_cols <- setdiff(names(H3_consumption), front_cols)
H3_consumption <- H3_consumption[, c(front_cols, other_cols)]

other_cols <- setdiff(names(H3_credit), front_cols)
H3_credit <- H3_credit[, c(front_cols, other_cols)]

table(H3_tax$na_item)
#We have 198 observations for direct and indirect taxation. We must make two separate
#columns for these using reshape()
H3_tax <- reshape(
  H3_tax,
  idvar = c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD"),  
  timevar = "na_item",       
  v.names = "OBS_VALUE",    
  direction = "wide"              
)

#rename the columns and remove sector column
H3_tax[4] <- (NULL)
names(H3_tax)[c(4,5)] <- c('indirect.tax.obs.value', 'direct.tax.obs.value')
#add new column for units
H3_tax$indirect.comment <- "taxes on production and imports, revenue"
H3_tax$direct.comment <- "taxes on income, wealth, etc, revenue"

#calculate the YoY change for both taxes
H3_tax$indirect.tax.obs.value <- ave(H3_tax$indirect.tax.obs.value, H3_tax$geo, 
                                 FUN = function(x) c(NA, diff(x)))

H3_tax$direct.tax.obs.value <- ave(H3_tax$direct.tax.obs.value, H3_tax$geo, 
                                 FUN = function(x) c(NA, diff(x)))

#change units
H3_tax$unit <- 'YoY.change.percent.of.gdp'

#create percentage change helper function for H3_primary_income
pct_change <- function(x) {
  lagged_x <- c(NA, x[-length(x)]) #shifts the vector down by 1 row
  return( ((x - lagged_x) / lagged_x) * 100 )
}

#find YoY change in primary income as a percentage, so (t - (t-1)) / (t-1)
H3_primary_income$primary.income.yoy.percent.change <- ave(
  H3_primary_income$OBS_VALUE, #replace with our exact column name
  H3_primary_income$geo,       #our country grouping column
  FUN = pct_change
)

H3_primary_income[5] <- (NULL)

#calculate YoY change for H3_credit. Units are already in percentage of GDP so
#only need to find the difference between each observed value each year
H3_credit <- H3_credit[order(H3_credit$geo, H3_credit$TIME_PERIOD), ]
#calculate YoY change grouped by country
H3_credit$yoy.change <- ave(H3_credit$OBS_VALUE, H3_credit$geo, 
                            FUN = function(x) c(NA, diff(x)))

#H3_property_prices is quarterly raw data where the unit is index, 2010=100.
#It also contains a bunch of unnecessary countries that must be removed
#and is in wide format that will have ti change to long format
H3_countries <- c('Austria', 'Belgium', 'Germany', 'Greece', 'Spain', 'Finland',
                  'France', 'Italy', 'Netherlands', 'Portugal')

H3_property_prices <- subset(H3_property_prices, Reference.area %in% H3_countries)

H3_property_prices_cols <- colnames(H3_property_prices)[-1]

H3_property_prices <- reshape(
  H3_property_prices,
  varying = H3_property_prices_cols,
  idvar = c("Reference.area"),    
  timevar = "quarter_raw",       
  v.names = "OBS_VALUE",    
  direction = "long"              
)

#match original column names to correct observed values (2019-Q3 to 138,556)
H3_property_prices$quarter_raw <- H3_property_prices_cols[H3_property_prices$quarter_raw]
rownames(H3_property_prices) <- NULL

#now calculate the annual average for each country for each year
#extract the 4-digit year from the quarter strings
H3_property_prices$Year <- as.numeric(substr(H3_property_prices$quarter_raw, 2, 5))

#calculate the mean of OBS_VALUE grouped by Reference.area and Year"
H3_property_prices$Annual_Mean <- ave(
  H3_property_prices$OBS_VALUE, 
  H3_property_prices$Reference.area, 
  H3_property_prices$Year, 
  FUN = mean
)

#now must calculate the YoY percentage change from the raw value annual means
#I will remove the quarterly raw values and only keep one row for each mean
#value and country instead of having four rows with the same mean values
H3_annual_property_prices <- unique(H3_property_prices[, c("Reference.area", "Year", "Annual_Mean")])

#sort chronologically by country and year
H3_annual_property_prices <- H3_annual_property_prices[order(H3_annual_property_prices$Reference.area, 
                                                             H3_annual_property_prices$Year), ]

#calculate YoY Pct Change (now looking back exactly 1 row)
H3_annual_property_prices$YoY_Pct_Change <- ave(
  H3_annual_property_prices$Annual_Mean, 
  H3_annual_property_prices$Reference.area, 
  FUN = function(x) {
    #compare each year to the row 1 year backward
    c(NA, diff(x) / x[-length(x)] * 100)
  }
)

rownames(H3_annual_property_prices) <- NULL
```

## EDA ON HYPOTHESIS 3 DATASETS

```{r include=FALSE}
#first remove Ireland from all datasets. I am not going to run multiple models
#with and without it only to find out it ruins my results like it did in the 
#previous hypotheses.

H3_tax <- H3_tax[H3_tax$geo != "Ireland", ]
H3_HICP_inflation <- H3_HICP_inflation[H3_HICP_inflation$geo != "Ireland", ]
H3_primary_income <- H3_primary_income[H3_primary_income$geo != "Ireland", ]
H3_credit <- H3_credit[H3_credit$geo != "Ireland", ]
H3_consumption <- H3_consumption[H3_consumption$geo != "Ireland", ]

#EDA ON H3_tax
nrow(H3_tax)
#190 observations
table(H3_tax$geo)
#every country has 19 observations, which is correct because I started at 2001
#instead of 2002 in order to still have 18 observations after calculating YoY change.
sum(is.na(H3_tax$indirect.tax.obs.value))
sum(is.na(H3_tax$direct.tax.obs.value))
#missing 10 values each, which is because we eliminate 2002 observations when
#calculating yoy and only have 10 countries instead of 11 
str(H3_tax)
summary(H3_tax$indirect.tax.obs.value)
#min value is -2.0, median is 0, mean is 0.063, max is 1.8
summary(H3_tax$direct.tax.obs.value)
#min is -2.3, median is 0, mean is 0.011, max is 2.4
colSums(is.na(H3_tax))
#only missing values in the yoy columns, 10 each

#remove 2001 rows because they have NA values
H3_tax <- H3_tax[H3_tax$TIME_PERIOD != 2001, ]
table(H3_tax$geo)
colSums(is.na(H3_tax))
#now have 18 observations per country, which is correct and no missing values
par(mfrow=c(1,1))
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
boxplot(indirect.tax.obs.value ~ geo,
        data=H3_tax,
        main='H3 YoY Change in Indirect Taxes',
        ylab='percentgae of GDP',
        xlab='country')
```

Since our units are in YoY percentage changes, all of the median values for each
country are close to 0 (as shown in the summary stats). However the variability 
differs by country, with the peripheral countries having the most variance, especially
Greece. Finland also has a lot of variability, about the same as Spain, but does 
not have the outliers that Spain has. Among the outliers, Belgium has a positive
outlier just above it's whisker, Germany has 3 outliers about the same distance
in each direction and is otherwise compact, Italy has 2 large positive outliers
exceeding 1% YoY change, Portugal has 2 large negative outlier exceeding a 1% 
YoY change, and finally Spain has 3 large outliers (1 positive and 2 negative).
This variability in the peripheral countries (excluding Ireland) in comparison to the
core Eurozone countries was expected, as these countries has to enact large tax
changes to stabilize their economies.

```{r echo=FALSE}
boxplot(direct.tax.obs.value ~ geo,
        data=H3_tax,
        main='H3 YoY Change in Direct Taxes',
        ylab='percentage of GDP',
        xlab='country')
```

Change in direct taxes shows larger variability in the core Eurozone countries 
compared to indirect taxation changes. The median changes among all countries is
still around 0, but each country now has much more variability and longer whiskers, 
with more outliers present, especially in the core countries. Greece, again, has 
the largest variability. However, there is not much difference between the peripheral 
countries and the core Eurozone countries. Austria, Belgium, France, and the Netherlands
now all have moderate outliers whereas they were much more compact with indirect taxes. 
There is a noticeable difference between the peripheral countries and the Eurozone 
countries in that the peripheral countries show more observations of increasing direct
taxes whereas the core countries show more observations reducing direct taxes.
This is expected given the peripheral countries were forced into austerity measures
that included tax hikes, whereas Germany and other more stable nations either kept 
taxation the same or cut fiscal spending.

```{r include=FALSE}
#EDA ON H3_HICP_inflation
nrow(H3_HICP_inflation)
#180 observations
table(H3_HICP_inflation$geo)
#every country has 18 observations, which is correct because I started at 2002
sum(is.na(H3_HICP_inflation$OBS_VALUE))
#no missing values
str(H3_HICP_inflation)
summary(H3_HICP_inflation$OBS_VALUE)
#min value is -1.4, median is 1.7, mean is 1.733, max is 4.7
colSums(is.na(H3_HICP_inflation))
#no missing values
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
boxplot(OBS_VALUE ~ geo,
```
