```{r echo=FALSE}
        data=H3_HICP_inflation,
        main='H3 YoY Change in HICP Inflation',
        ylab='yearly inflation change',
        xlab='country')
```

Greece, Spain, Italy, and Portugal all have the most variability compared to all 
other nations, as expected. Greece, Spain, and Portugal all experienced deflation
from lack of consumer demand from rising unemployment and wage cuts, which is shown
in their longer negative whiskers and IQR. These countries also experienced large 
inflation through rising indirect taxation (VAT).
The core countries did not experience deflation, but did reach close to or surpass
3% YoY changes, with Belgium observing a YoY difference of 4.5%. Given the core
Eurozone countries were more resilient and did not have to impose severe austerity
measures, their YoY inflation variability is far less compared to peripheral countries.

```{r include=FALSE}
#EDA ON H3_primary_income
nrow(H3_primary_income)
#190 observations
table(H3_primary_income$geo)
#every country has 19 observations, which is correct
sum(is.na(H3_primary_income$primary.income.yoy.percent.change))
#missing 10 values each, which is because we eliminate 2002 observations when
#calculating yoy and only have 10 countries instead of 11 
str(H3_primary_income)
summary(H3_primary_income$primary.income.yoy.percent.change)
#min value is -9.579, median is 2.798, mean is 2.447, max is 10.434
colSums(is.na(H3_primary_income))
#only missing values in the yoy columns, 10 total, one for each country

#remove 2001 rows because they have NA values from the YoY change calc
H3_primary_income <- H3_primary_income[H3_primary_income$TIME_PERIOD != 2001, ]
table(H3_primary_income$geo)
colSums(is.na(H3_primary_income))
#now have 18 observations per country, which is correct since this variable is not
#lagged, so these are the final observations. The other variables with 18 observations
#still need to be lagged so they will have 17 in final dataset. There are also no missing values
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
boxplot(primary.income.yoy.percent.change ~ geo,
        data=H3_primary_income,
        main='H3 YoY Change in Primary Income',
        ylab='current prices, million of euros',
        xlab='country')
```

All median values are positive, but as expected there is a substantial difference
between core Eurozone countries and the peripheral countries. Greece is by far the most
variable, ranging in observations from +10% to -9% in YoY primary income changes.
Spain, Portugal, and Italy all have large negative observations. Germany and France 
both have one negative outlier that is around -2%. None of the core Eurozone countries
experienced a negative decline in YoY primary income changes greater than 2%, with
most observations being above 0.

```{r include=FALSE}
#EDA ON H3_annual_property_prices
nrow(H3_annual_property_prices)
#190 observations
table(H3_annual_property_prices$Reference.area)
#every country has 19 observations, which is correct because I started at 2001
#instead of 2002 in order to still have 18 observations after calculating YoY change.
sum(is.na(H3_annual_property_prices$YoY_Pct_Change))
#missing 10 values each, which is because we eliminate 2002 observations when
#calculating yoy and only have 10 countries instead of 11 
str(H3_annual_property_prices)
summary(H3_annual_property_prices$YoY_Pct_Change)
#min value is -16.8, median is 0.961, mean is 1.16, max is 14.135
colSums(is.na(H3_annual_property_prices))
#no missing values except for YoY change, which is 10

#remove 2001 rows because they have NA values
H3_annual_property_prices <- H3_annual_property_prices[H3_annual_property_prices$Year != 2001, ]
table(H3_annual_property_prices$Reference.area)
colSums(is.na(H3_annual_property_prices))
#now have 18 observations per country, which is correct and no missing values
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
boxplot(YoY_Pct_Change ~ Reference.area,
        data=H3_annual_property_prices,
        main='H3 YoY Change in Median Annual Property Prices',
        ylab='YoY percent change in median prices',
        xlab='country')
```

All countries have positive median YoY changes except for Greece and Italy.
Spain has the largest variability, which is expected given it had a large real estate
bubble prior to the debt crisis. Portugal also experienced a large decrease in 
YoY property values and so did the Netherlands. France shows the largest YoY increase
in property values, on par with Spain. The core Eurozone countries show much more
stability (less variability), although Austria does have the only 2 outliers, one 
positive and one negative.

```{r include=FALSE}
#EDA ON H3_credit
nrow(H3_credit)
#190 observations
table(H3_credit$geo)
#every country has 19 observations, which is correct because I started at 2001
#instead of 2002 in order to still have 18 observations after calculating YoY change.
sum(is.na(H3_credit$yoy.change))
#10 missing values because I removed observation from 2001 when calculating YoY 
str(H3_credit)
summary(H3_credit$yoy.change)
#min value is -26.4, median is 1.35, mean is 1.501, max is 32.2
colSums(is.na(H3_credit))
#only missing values in the yoy columns, 10 each

#remove 2001 rows because they have NA values
H3_credit <- H3_credit[H3_credit$TIME_PERIOD != 2001, ]
table(H3_credit$geo)
colSums(is.na(H3_credit))
#now have 18 observations per country, which is correct and no missing values
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
boxplot(yoy.change ~ geo,
        data=H3_credit,
        main='H3 YoY Change in Credit',
        ylab = 'percent of GDP',
        xlab='country')
```

Outside of the expected peripheral countries being hit hardest by credit crunch,
Belgium and the Netherlands also experienced a lot fo variability. Spain and Austria 
are the only two countries with negative median YoY change, although Spain is a couple
of percentage points larger than Austria. France, Germany, and Italy all had the
least variability in YoY credit changes, which is expected since German banks were
predominately the ones financing the credit boom in other Eurozone countries.

```{r include=FALSE}
#EDA ON H3_consumption
nrow(H3_consumption)
#180 observations
table(H3_consumption$geo)
#every country has 18 observations, which is correct because I started at 2002
sum(is.na(H3_consumption$OBS_VALUE))
#no missing values
str(H3_consumption)
summary(H3_consumption$OBS_VALUE)
#min value is -9.4, median is 1.2, mean is 0.987, max is 5.9
colSums(is.na(H3_consumption))
#no missing values
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
boxplot(OBS_VALUE ~ geo,
        data=H3_consumption,
        main='H3 YoY Change in Household Consumption Expenditure',
        ylab= 'final consumption expenditure of households',
        xlab='country')
```

As expected, Greece had the largest negative household consumption YoY change, 
approaching 10%. It also had the largest positive change over 5%. Spain, Portugal,
and Italy all have significant decreases compared to the other countries. Finland
has a large decrease as well. Italy and Portugal have the only negative outliers,
with all 3 outliers being significantly larger than each country's respective 
negative whisker. Belgium has the only positive outlier and it is significantly
larger than its positive whisker. All of the means for each country are positive.
The core Eurozone countries did not have large variances from their means, with 
most core countries experiencing mostly positive growth and slightly negative 
observations.

```{r include=FALSE}
#_______________________________________________________________________________

#H3 MERGE ALL DATASETS

#_______________________________________________________________________________


#recheck all datasets
nrow(H3_tax) #180
nrow(H3_credit) #180
nrow(H3_consumption) #180
nrow(H3_primary_income) #180
nrow(H3_annual_property_prices) #180
nrow(H3_HICP_inflation) #180

table(H3_tax$geo)
table(H3_credit$geo)
table(H3_consumption$geo)
table(H3_primary_income$geo)
table(H3_annual_property_prices$Reference.area) 
table(H3_HICP_inflation$geo)
#all datasets have the same 10 countries and 180 observations since we excluded Ireland. 

#MERGE ALL H3 DATASETS

#remove unnecessary columns
H3_credit[, c("co_nco", "finpos")] <- list(NULL)

#rename columns of each dataset
names(H3_tax)[3] <- c('tax.unit')
names(H3_credit)[c(3,4,5,6,7)] <- c('credit.unit', 'credit.obs.value', 'credit.sector',
                                    'credit.comment', 'credit.yoy.change')

names(H3_consumption)[c(3,4,5)] <- c('consumption.unit', 'consumption.obs.value', 
                                     'consumption.comment')

names(H3_primary_income)[c(3,4,5,6)] <- c('primary.income.unit', 'primary.income.obs.value',
                                               'primary.income.comment', 'primary.income.sector')

names(H3_annual_property_prices)[c(1,2,3,4)] <- c('geo', 'TIME_PERIOD', 'property.annual.mean',
                                                  'property.yoy.percent.change')

names(H3_HICP_inflation)[c(3,4,5)] <- c('HICP.unit', 'HICP.obs.value', 'HICP.comment')

#make list of datasets to merge
dataset_list <- list(H3_consumption, H3_tax, H3_credit, H3_primary_income, 
                     H3_annual_property_prices, H3_HICP_inflation)

H3_final <- Reduce(function(x, y) merge(x, y, by = c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD"), all = TRUE), 
                   dataset_list)

nrow(H3_final)
#180 rows
table(H3_final$geo)
#all countries included with 18 observations each


#______________________________________________________________________________

#H3 LAG NECESSARY VARIABLES IN EACH DATASET

#_______________________________________________________________________________

```

## HYPOTHESIS 3 PREDICTOR LAGS

Taxation (direct and indirect) are lagged by 1 year (t-1) because changes in tax
policy do not alter aggregate consumer behavior overnight. While a VAT hike is
salient at the point of sale, the full adjustment of household consumption bundles,
contract re-negotiations, and behavioral shifts unfolds over several quarters.
Total tax revenues over GDP are inherently endogenous to current-period consumption
For example, a sudden drop in consumption in year t would automatically lower
indirect tax revenues in year t. Lagging the tax-to-GDP ratios ensures that we
are measuring the impact of past fiscal policy choices on subsequent consumption
growth, preventing reverse causality.

Credit conditions is lagged by 1 year (t-1). Credit crunches, banking distress,
and the resulting shift toward precautionary savings operate with a lag.
A tightening of credit lines or a banking scare in year t-1 restricts borrowing
capacity and heightens consumer anxiety, which manifests as a contraction in retail
shopping and consumption growth in year t.

Household Wealth in residential property is lagged by 1 year (t-1). The wealth
effect (or its destruction, as seen in the Spanish housing crash) takes time
to filter through to everyday retail spending. Homeowners do not immediately
slash baseline consumption the month property values drop; rather, the realization
of eroded equity and reduced borrowing power against housing assets depresses
consumption growth over the subsequent year.

Gross Primary Income Growth is unlagged (t). Current consumption is strictly
bound by current income constraints. If a household’s primary income drops
in year t, their ability to spend is instantly curtailed. Leaving this unlagged
ensures we are truly holding "actual household spending power" constant in the
exact period consumption is measured. This cleanly isolates whether the composition
of the tax hike (from the previous year) causes a differential drop in consumption
independent of current income fluctuations.

HICP Inflation is unlagged (t) because inflation directly alters the real value
of a consumer's money in real-time. Immediate price shocks force instant trade-offs
in nominal spending and volume of goods purchased. If inflation were lagged,
the model would fail to control for current-period price distortions, leaving the
current consumption growth rate heavily confounded by immediate changes in the
cost of living.

In summary, by lagging the tax policy and wealth variables, the model captures
the behavioral adjustment period and protects against reverse causality
(where current consumption shifts drive tax revenues or property values).
Conversely, by keeping income and inflation concurrent, the model establishes
a strict, real-time baseline of a household's contemporaneous purchasing power.
This asymmetry directly serves the core goal: it allows us to test whether
a past shift toward highly salient indirect taxation triggers a sharper behavioral
pullback in retail shopping than a shift toward direct taxation.

```{r include=FALSE}
#ensure that rows are in the exact linear sequence required for lagging.
H3_final <- H3_final[order(H3_final$geo, H3_final$TIME_PERIOD), ]

#drop the last observation of a vector and shift everything down by one
lag <- function(x) {return(c(NA, x[-length(x)]))}

#apply the lag to every country and create new columns
H3_final$lag.tax.indirect <- ave(H3_final$indirect.tax.obs.value, H3_final$geo, FUN = lag)
H3_final$lag.tax.direct <- ave(H3_final$direct.tax.obs.value, H3_final$geo, FUN = lag)
H3_final$lag.credit <- ave(H3_final$credit.yoy.change, H3_final$geo, FUN = lag)
H3_final$lag.property <- ave(H3_final$property.yoy.percent.change, H3_final$geo, FUN = lag)

#check it worked
verification_fields <- c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD", "direct.tax.obs.value", "lag.tax.direct", 
                         "property.yoy.percent.change", "lag.property")

print(H3_final[24:50, verification_fields], row.names = FALSE)
#everything looks good

#remove 2002 column
H3_final <- H3_final[H3_final$TIME_PERIOD != 2002, ]
nrow(H3_final) #170 observations
table(H3_final$geo) #17 observations per country
sum(is.na(H3_final)) #no missing values
```

## HYPOTHESIS 3 ASSUMPTION CHECKS

Our Hypothesis 3 model

```{r echo=TRUE}
H3_plm <- plm(consumption.obs.value ~ lag.tax.indirect + lag.tax.direct + lag.credit +
                                  lag.property + primary.income.yoy.percent.change + 
                                  HICP.obs.value,
                               data = H3_final,
                               index = c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD"),
                               model = "within", #within = fixed effects
                               effect = "twoways")
```

### MULTICOLLINEARITY CHECK

```{r include=FALSE}
#extract the within-transformed (demeaned) variables properly
transformed_X <- as.data.frame(model.matrix(H3_plm))
transformed_Y <- as.numeric(pmodel.response(H3_plm))

#bind them together into a temporary data frame
vif_data <- cbind(transformed_Y, transformed_X)

#run standard lm() because R now sees them as completely separate columns
pure_vif_check <- lm(transformed_Y ~ ., data = vif_data)
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
vif(pure_vif_check)
# > vif(pure_vif_check)
# lag.tax.indirect                  lag.tax.direct                    lag.credit 
# 1.025637                          1.053634                          1.040590 
# lag.property          primary.income.yoy.percent.change             HICP.obs.value 
# 1.426192                          1.342513                          1.167340 
```

All predictors have very low multicollinearity. This was done by treating 
the VIF check the same way the plm model handles the data, resulting in the 
isolated variations in each predictor.

### LINEARITY CHECK

```{r include=FALSE}
#extract the clean, model-transformed dependent variable (excludes dropped rows)
actual_Y <- as.numeric(pmodel.response(H3_plm))

#extract the model residuals
resids <- as.numeric(residuals(H3_plm))

#calculate fitted values using matching dimensions
fitted_values <- actual_Y - resids
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
plot(fitted_values, resids, 
     main = "H3 ex Ireland - Residuals vs Fitted", 
     xlab = "Fitted Values", ylab = "Residuals",
     pch = 19, col = rgb(0, 0, 0, 0.5))
abline(h = 0, col = "red", lwd = 2)

#LOESS smooth trend line
lines(lowess(fitted_values, resids, f=0.75), col = "blue", lwd = 2)
```

All residuals are evenly scattered above and below 0. Our blue LOESS trend line is 
straight with no curve and stays close to 0 within the main cloud of points. 
There is no shape or pattern in the residuals. Our LOESS trend line does shift
downward after the -2 fitted value mark from the points that have large negative
residuals and large negative fitted values. However, this is fine because the 
trend line is straight and close to 0 in the main cloud of points, and being 
deviated from this by a small amount of outlier values does not ruin the assumption.

There are some residuals that have much higher variance, so homoscedasticity may not 
be met, but it is only for a small amount of residuals, as most residuals are contained
between -2 and 2 on the y-axis. I will run a formal Breusch-Pagan test to check 
homoscedasticity and apply Driscoll-Kraay standard errors regardless of the results
to further protect my model's standard errors.

As for the spread on the x-axis, there are some outlier fitted values on the right 
and left, especially the left, but it is only a handful of values. I will create a 
residual vs leverage plot to make sure these points are not strongly influencing
my regression model.
Overall, I will say linearity is met.

### INDEPENDENCE CHECK

Test for Autocorrelation within countries
H0: errors are independent (no autocorrelation)
H1: errors are not independent (autocorrelation exists)

```{r echo=FALSE}
pbgtest(H3_plm) #Breusch-Godfrey test for panel data
# > pbgtest(H3_plm) #Breusch-Godfrey test for panel data
# 
# Breusch-Godfrey/Wooldridge test for serial correlation in panel models
# 
# data:  consumption.obs.value ~ lag.tax.indirect + lag.tax.direct + lag.credit +  ...
# chisq = 36.746, df = 17, p-value = 0.003642
# alternative hypothesis: serial correlation in idiosyncratic errors
```

We reject H0 and conclude that our errors are not independent and suffer from
autocorrelation.

Test for Cross-Sectional Dependence (Spatial correlation between countries)
H0: errors are independent
H1: errors are not independent

```{r echo=FALSE}
pcdtest(H3_plm, test = "lm")
# > pcdtest(H3_plm, test = "lm")
# 
# Breusch-Pagan LM test for cross-sectional dependence in panels
# 
# data:  consumption.obs.value ~ lag.tax.indirect + lag.tax.direct + lag.credit +     lag.property + primary.income.yoy.percent.change + HICP.obs.value
# chisq = 66.467, df = 45, p-value = 0.02035
# alternative hypothesis: cross-sectional dependence
```

We reject H0 and conclude that errors are not independent between countries.

Since autocorrelation is present within countries and between countries,
we apply Driscoll-Kraay standard errors.

### NORMALITY CHECK

```{r echo=FALSE}
#extract clean numeric residuals
resids <- as.numeric(residuals(H3_plm))

qqnorm(resids, main = "H3 ex Ireland - QQ Plot of Panel Residuals")
qqline(resids, col = "red")
```

Our QQ plot shows that the panel residuals track the theoretical normal 
distribution closely between the -1.5 to 1.5 quantiles. Beyond this, the plot 
shows heavy tails on both ends, although they do not create a distinct S-shape.
Regardless, this indicates large residuals exist that deviate from a strict normal
distribution. The heavy-tails are expected given severe systemic economic crisis 
and structural shocks. However, with a total panel size of 170 observations, the
Central Limit Theorem ensures that the distribution of our coefficient estimates is 
asymptotically normal. Therefore, despite the non-normal distributions present 
in the extreme tails of the raw residuals, we can confidently rely on our standard 
errors and final inferential results. 
Overall, while the strict assumption of residual normality is technically violated
in the tails due to predictable crisis-driven outliers, the asymptotic properties 
of our sample size render the model highly robust and statistically valid.

### EQUAL VARIANCE CHECK

H0: errors have equal variance
H1: errors do not have equal variance

```{r echo=FALSE}
bptest(H3_plm)
# > bptest(H3_plm)
# 
# studentized Breusch-Pagan test
# 
# data:  H3_plm
# BP = 14.139, df = 6, p-value = 0.02812
```

Since out p-value is 0.028, this is less than our significance level of 0.05,
so we reject H0 and conclude that our errors do not have equal variance. 
It is understandable why our model failed equal variance assumption since
a 1% increase in fiscal tightening can cause predictable responses in one country 
but highly volatile responses in another. Furthermore, the impact of the crisis was
highly unequal among the included nations, with the peripheral countries ultimately 
receiving the most economic impact relative to the core Eurozone countries.

We implement Driscoll-Kraay (SCC) standard errors to ensure that the final significance
testing and confidence intervals for our expenditure and revenue predictors are
statistically conservative.

### LEVERAGE AND COOK'S DISTANCE CHECK EX IRELAND

```{r include=FALSE}
#reset row names to sequence from 1 to the total number of rows
rownames(H3_final) <- NULL
#use least squares dummy variable 
H3_lsdv <- lm(consumption.obs.value ~ lag.tax.indirect + lag.tax.direct +
                lag.credit + lag.property + HICP.obs.value + primary.income.yoy.percent.change +
                factor(geo) + factor(TIME_PERIOD), data = H3_final)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
#Residuals vs Leverage plot
plot(H3_lsdv, which = 5, main = "H3 ex Ireland - Residuals vs Leverage")

#Cook's Distance by Observation Index
cooks_vals <- cooks.distance(H3_lsdv)
plot(cooks_vals, type = "h", main = "Cook's Distance", ylab = "Cook's D Value")

# Add a horizontal line for the 4/n threshold
abline(h = 4 / nrow(H3_final), col = "red", lty = 2)

#find the country and year for our top leverage points
H3_final[c(88, 95, 163), c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD")]
# > H3_final[c(88, 95, 163), c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD")]
# geo TIME_PERIOD
# 88  Greece        2005
# 95  Greece        2012
# 163  Spain        2012
```

Observation 88 (Greece, 2005): This data point is a high residual, moderate leverage
outlier with a standardized residual above +3 and near 0.3. This indicates that
the YoY household consumption growth rate in 2005 was roughly three standard deviations
higher than what the model’s predictors (lagged tax adjustments, inflation, primary
income growth, and private credit shifts) anticipated. The massive pre-crisis influx
of cheap credit and cheap borrowing post-Euro entry triggered a domestic demand
and consumption boom that completely outpaced the structural fundamentals of the
baseline Eurozone trend.

Observation 95 (Greece, 2012): This is a high residual, moderate leverage outlier,
with a standardized residual approaching -4 and leverage over 0.3. 2012 represents
the highest point of Greece’s structural adjustment crisis. Following the implementation
of severe wage suppression, public sector layoffs, and aggressive consumption tax hikes
(VAT increases), household purchasing power collapsed. The model significantly
under predicted the depth of this contraction, making this a pivotal observation for
capturing the acute demand destruction caused by austerity.

Observation 163 (Spain, 2012): This data point is a high residual, moderate leverage
outlier with a residual of approximately +2.5 and moderate leverage near 0.18. While Spain was
involved in a severe housing bubble collapse and credit market contraction, its positive
vertical positioning means the model significantly under predicted household consumption
growth for this year. This cleanly captures the intra-year demand distortion caused
by anticipatory consumer hoarding behavior directly ahead of Spain's massive September
2012 statutory VAT hike.

The Residuals vs Leverage plot additionally reveals an extreme-leverage observation
on the far right of the horizontal axis (leverage approx. 0.45) carrying a moderate
negative residual (approx. -1.5). While this data point pushes the leverage baseline
outward, it sits comfortably within the standard Cook's Distance boundary lines,
meaning its structural distance does not negatively bias the overall trend.

The Case for Keeping Greece 2005 and 2012: These points represent authentic, real-world
behavioral responses to extreme shifts in economic environments. Removing them
would mask the extreme consumer demand reactions that occur during a debt crisis.
Their elevated Cook's Distance represents necessary variation to cleanly evaluate
how households alter spending when facing direct income tax changes versus indirect
VAT hikes.

The Case for Keeping Spain 2009: While Spain 2009 exhibits high leverage, it is
"good leverage" because it demonstrates that our household wealth and credit
market controls are working exactly as intended. Retaining Spain 2009 allows the
model to successfully "soak up" the independent consumption drag caused by a banking
and real estate collapse, preventing those non-fiscal shocks from incorrectly bleeding
into and biasing our direct and indirect tax coefficients.

## HYPOTHESIS 3 CONSUMPTION ANALYSIS

```{r include=FALSE}
#apply Driscoll-Kraay Standard Errors to H2 plm model
H3_final_results <- coeftest(H3_plm, 
                             vcov = vcovSCC(H3_plm, 
                                            type = "HC3", 
                                            cluster = "time"))
```

Null Hypothesis 3: Holding total household primary income, household wealth, and credit 
conditions constant, the specific mix of fiscal consolidation (Direct vs. Indirect
taxation) has no differential impact on household consumption growth
(beta_indirect = beta_direct) .

Alternative Hypothesis 3: For an equivalent reduction in purchasing power, shifting 
the tax burden toward indirect taxation (VAT) causes a significantly sharper contraction 
in household consumption growth than direct taxation (beta_indirect != beta_direct),
because consumption taxes are highly salient at the point of sale and disproportionately
target households with a higher marginal propensity to consume (MPC). 

```{r echo=FALSE}
print(H3_final_results)
# > print(H3_final_results)
# 
# t test of coefficients:
#   
#                                   Estimate Std. Error t value  Pr(>|t|)    
# lag.tax.indirect                  -0.150762   0.276524 -0.5452  0.586493    
# lag.tax.direct                    -0.079310   0.204970 -0.3869  0.699401    
# lag.credit                         0.016729   0.011163  1.4986  0.136260    
# lag.property                       0.046760   0.016102  2.9041  0.004291 ** 
# primary.income.yoy.percent.change  0.594874   0.071956  8.2672 1.022e-13 ***
# HICP.obs.value                    -0.503226   0.124208 -4.0515 8.455e-05 ***
#   ---
#   Signif. codes:  0 ‘***’ 0.001 ‘**’ 0.01 ‘*’ 0.05 ‘.’ 0.1 ‘ ’ 1
```

Lagged Indirect Taxes over GDP (lag.tax.indirect):
The Coefficient is -0.1508 with a p-value of p = 0.586. Holding all else constant,
a 1 percentage point increase in the lagged change in indirect taxes over GDP
is associated with a 0.151 percentage point decrease in household consumption
growth the following year. The p-value remains well above our significance level
of 0.05, so we fail to reject H0 at the individual level and conclude that
indirect tax changes in year t-1 have a statistically insignificant relationship
with the household consumption growth trajectory in the following year t.
From an isolated policy standpoint, raising or lowering consumer sales taxes (VAT)
does not show a statistically reliable, direct historical contractionary effect
on next-year consumption growth once Two-Way Fixed Effects are accounted for.

Lagged Direct Taxes over GDP (lag.tax.direct):
The Coefficient is -0.0793 with a p-value of p = 0.699. Holding all else constant,
a 1 percentage point increase in the lagged change in direct taxes over GDP is
associated with a 0.079 percentage point decrease in household consumption growth
the following year. Because the p-value is well above our significance level of
0.05, it is statistically insignificant, meaning direct tax fluctuations do not
have a reliable independent impact on subsequent spending growth. While beta_indirect (-0.151)
is mathematically nearly twice as contractionary as beta_direct (-0.0793) aligning
with our theoretical expectations of higher VAT salience and MPC targets, neither
variable achieves individual statistical significance.

Lagged Credit to the Private Sector over GDP (lag.credit):
The Coefficient is 0.0167 with a p-value of p = 0.136. Holding all else constant,
a 1 percentage point increase in the lagged change in private credit as a percentage
of GDP is associated with a 0.017 percentage point increase in household
consumption growth the following year. The purpose of this variable is to isolate
the credit-market freezing and precautionary savings spikes that defined the banking
collapse era. Although it remains statistically insignificant at the 0.05 level within
this two-way demeaning framework, its presence in the model remains a critical
structural control to prevent banking-health shocks from biasing our primary tax
coefficients.

Lagged Real Residential Property Price Index (lag.property):
The Coefficient is 0.0468 with a p-value of p = 0.004. Holding all else constant,
a 1 percentage point increase in the real residential property price growth rate
in year t-1 is associated with a 0.047 percentage point increase in household
consumption growth in year t. The p-value is highly statistically significant,
providing strong empirical proof of a powerful wealth effect channel across the
Eurozone. This coefficient confirms that changes in housing wealth, such as the
massive household balance sheet destruction seen during the Spanish property bubble
bursts, exert a dominant, lingering influence on everyday retail shopping behavior.

Gross Primary Income Growth YoY (primary.income.yoy.percent.change):
The Coefficient is 0.5949 with a p-value of p < 0.001. Holding all else constant,
a 1 percentage point increase in contemporaneous gross primary income growth
is associated with a 0.595 percentage point increase in household consumption
growth in the same year. This relationship is highly statistically significant
and represents the largest positive magnitude in the model. By design, this
unlagged control captures the immediate, mechanical transmission of household
spending power. Including this ensures that our tax coefficients are strictly
isolating the behavioral and salience responses of fiscal policy, rather than
merely capturing the baseline income effect.

HICP Inflation YoY (HICP.obs.value):
The Coefficient is -0.5032 with a p-value of p < 0.001. Holding all else constant,
a 1 percentage point increase in the unlagged HICP inflation rate is associated
with a 0.503 percentage point decrease in household consumption growth. This
coefficient is highly statistically significant, establishing that sharp increases
in consumer prices severely erode purchasing power and immediately suppress
retail shopping volume. By capturing unique, country-specific price deviations
from the Eurozone baseline, this control ensures that inflation shocks do not
conflate our interpretation of nominal tax policy changes.

Formal test whether spending cuts and tax hikes have equal impacts on unemployment

```{r include=FALSE}
H3_hypothesis_test <- linearHypothesis(H3_plm, 
                                       "lag.tax.indirect = lag.tax.direct", 
                                       vcov = vcovSCC(H3_plm, 
                                                      type = "HC3", 
                                                      cluster = "time"))
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
print(H3_hypothesis_test)
# > print(H3_hypothesis_test)
# 
# Linear hypothesis test:
#   lag.tax.indirect - lag.tax.direct = 0
# 
# Model 1: restricted model
# Model 2: consumption.obs.value ~ lag.tax.indirect + lag.tax.direct + lag.credit + 
#   lag.property + primary.income.yoy.percent.change + HICP.obs.value
# 
# Note: Coefficient covariance matrix supplied.
# 
# Res.Df Df  Chisq Pr(>Chisq)
# 1    139                     
# 2    138  1 0.0488     0.8251
```

OVERALL HYPOTHESIS 3 CONCLUSION FOR HOUSEHOLD CONSUMPTION GROWTH EX IRELAND:
While the individual coefficient testing showed a mathematically stronger negative
impact for indirect taxation (beta = -0.1508, p = 0.586) than for direct taxation
(beta = -0.0793, p = 0.699), a formal Linear Restriction Test (Wald Test) was
executed to directly evaluate the restriction lag.tax.indirect - lag.tax.direct = 0.
The test yields a Chi-squared statistic of 0.0488 with a p-value of 0.8251.
Because this p-value is vastly higher than our 0.05 significance threshold,
we fail to reject Null Hypothesis 3. Therefore, we cannot statistically conclude
that shifting the fiscal consolidation burden toward indirect taxation (VAT)
causes a significantly sharper contraction in household consumption growth than
direct taxation for an equivalent reduction in purchasing power. The underlying
driver of this result is the high degree of statistical noise in our fiscal policy
estimators. The standard errors for both lag.tax.indirect (0.2765) and lag.tax.direct
(0.2050) are very large relative to their point estimates. Because the
model faces considerable uncertainty regarding the true independent trajectories
of these tax types within the restricted panel, their respective confidence
intervals are highly expanded and overlap extensively. This prevents any statistical
differentiation between the two mechanisms. Furthermore, isolating this panel
framework by dropping Ireland and layering a Two-Way Within transformation
(Country and Year Fixed Effects) strips out a large amount of cross-sectional
and temporal variation. While this is necessary to eliminate omitted
variable bias and baseline macro trends, it leaves the model relying on highly
specific, idiosyncratic policy variations. With a small group of Eurozone nations
over a tight timeline, the Wald test demands a very high threshold of
structural divergence to declare two policy coefficients distinct from one another.
Consequently, the theoretical mechanisms of tax salience and marginal propensity
to consume (MPC) cannot be statistically confirmed in this specification.

<div style="page-break-after: always;"></div>

# HYPOTHESIS 4 ABSTRACT AND METHODOLOGY

Null Hypothesis 4: Holding the total scale of fiscal consolidation constant
(raising the exact same amount of total government revenue), the specific mix of
taxation adjustments (relying on Indirect Sales Taxes/VAT vs. Direct Income Taxes)
has no significant relationship with shifts in the S80/S20 income inequality ratio.
(beta_TaxMix = 0)

Alternative Hypothesis 4: The tax mix chosen for budget consolidation has a significant
linear relationship with income inequality, meaning that a heavy reliance on indirect
consumption taxes increases the rich-poor gap due to its regressive impact on 
lower-income households. (beta_TaxMix != 0)

This section evaluates the distributional consequences of the revenue-generation
side of austerity, examining whether balancing national budgets via consumption
tax hikes exacerbates domestic income inequality. It tests whether an intentional
shift in the tax mix toward regressive indirect taxes (VAT) expands the rich-poor
gap when holding the total volume of fiscal consolidation and welfare expenditures
constant. The design implements an intricate, dual-timeline lagging framework to
correct for the structural one-year data-gathering delay inherent to the EU-SILC
survey design. By exploiting within-country policy variations over an adjusted
temporal window, the model determines whether the structural selection of revenue
instruments breaks neutrality regarding household income distribution.

Dependent Variable YoY change in the S80/S20 Income Quintile Share Ratio:
First-differencing captures the annual velocity of income divergence.

Predictor YoY change in indirect taxes:
Measured as a percentage of total tax revenues, calculated as
indirect tax / (indirect + direct + net social contributions + capital tax).
Controls for regressive consumption taxes to systematically drain low-income
disposable bundles. Omitting the direct tax share avoids perfect multicollinearity.

Predictor YoY change in total general government revenue:
Measured as a percentage of potential GDP. This holds the total scale of revenue-side 
fiscal consolidation constant, isolating the compositional effect of the tax mix 
from the volume effect.

Predictor YoY change in net general government expenditure on social protection 
and health:
Measured as a percentage of potential GDP. This isolates spending-side safety net 
cuts, ensuring the tax mix coefficient does not absorb simultaneous austerity welfare 
pullbacks.

Predictor YoY change in Output Gap:
Measured as a percentage of potential GDP. Controls for the economic momentum
of the previous year.

Predictor YoY change in Total Unemployment:
Controls for labor market distress associated with the timing of the fiscal
consolidation.

Predictor YoY real GDP growth rate:
This control for broad growth baseline concurrent to the actual income generation
year, holding contemporaneous macroeconomic health constant.

```{r include=FALSE}
#_______________________________________________________________________________

#H4 IMPORT DATA AND CLEAN

#_______________________________________________________________________________


H4_inequality_ratio <- read.csv(here('data', 'H4_S80_S20.csv'))
H4_tax_rev <- read.csv(here('data', 'H4_tax_revenue.csv'))
H4_social <- read.csv(here('data', 'H4_social_protections.csv'))
H4_unemployment <- read.csv(here('data', 'H4_unemployment.csv'))
H4_potential_gdp <- read.csv(here('data', 'H4_potential_GDP.csv'))
H4_real_GDP_growth <- read.csv(here('data', 'H4_real_GDP_growth_rate.csv'))

#remove unnecessary columns for all eurostat datasets and rearrange 
cols_remove_eurostat <- c('DATAFLOW', 'LAST.UPDATE', 'OBS_FLAG', 'CONF_STATUS', 'freq')
cols_remove_potential <- c('FREQ', 'TRANSFORMATION', 'AGG_METHOD', 'REFERENCE',
                           'OBS_STATUS', 'OBS_CONF', 'UNIT_MULT', 'DATAFLOW', 'LAST.UPDATE')
H4_inequality_ratio[cols_remove_eurostat] <- list(NULL)
H4_tax_rev[cols_remove_eurostat] <- list(NULL)
H4_social[cols_remove_eurostat] <- list(NULL)
H4_unemployment[cols_remove_eurostat] <- list(NULL)
H4_potential_gdp[cols_remove_potential] <- list(NULL)
H4_real_GDP_growth[cols_remove_eurostat] <- list(NULL)

#change REF_AREA to geo for H4_potential
colnames(H4_potential_gdp)[colnames(H4_potential_gdp) == "REF_AREA"] <- "geo"
colnames(H4_potential_gdp)[colnames(H4_potential_gdp) == "UNIT"] <- "unit"
#rearrange columns so that main columns of interest are listed first
front_cols <- c('geo', 'TIME_PERIOD', 'unit', 'OBS_VALUE')
#find all the remaining columns
other_cols <- setdiff(names(H4_inequality_ratio), front_cols)
H4_inequality_ratio <- H4_inequality_ratio[, c(front_cols, other_cols)]

other_cols <- setdiff(names(H4_tax_rev), front_cols)
H4_tax_rev <- H4_tax_rev[, c(front_cols, other_cols)]

other_cols <- setdiff(names(H4_social), front_cols)
H4_social <- H4_social[, c(front_cols, other_cols)]

other_cols <- setdiff(names(H4_unemployment), front_cols)
H4_unemployment <- H4_unemployment[, c(front_cols, other_cols)]

other_cols <- setdiff(names(H4_potential_gdp), front_cols)
H4_potential_gdp <- H4_potential_gdp[, c(front_cols, other_cols)]

other_cols <- setdiff(names(H4_real_GDP_growth), front_cols)
H4_real_GDP_growth <- H4_real_GDP_growth[, c(front_cols, other_cols)]

table(H4_inequality_ratio$geo)
#We are missing 2 observations for Netherlands and Germany, and 1 observation
#for Finland, Italy, Portugal, and Spain
table(H4_inequality_ratio$TIME_PERIOD)
#missing 7 observations from 2003 and 2 from 2004. I will have to remove 2003 
#completely from lack of data since the dependent variable is YoY change and we
#cant calculate that using limited 2003 data. So we will have a reduced sample
#since every country will either be starting at 2005 or 2006.

#remove rows with 2003
H4_inequality_ratio <- H4_inequality_ratio[H4_inequality_ratio$TIME_PERIOD != "2003", ]
#find YoY change in income inequality ratio
H4_inequality_ratio <- H4_inequality_ratio[order(H4_inequality_ratio$geo, 
                                                 H4_inequality_ratio$TIME_PERIOD), ]

H4_inequality_ratio$yoy.inequality <- ave(H4_inequality_ratio$OBS_VALUE, H4_inequality_ratio$geo, 
                                          FUN = function(x) {return(c(NA, diff(x)))})
#note that a value in 2005 in actually the YoY change from 2003 to 2004, since the 
#survey is released a year late.

table(H4_tax_rev$geo) #200 obs for each country
table(H4_tax_rev$TIME_PERIOD) #100 obs for each year 2000-2019
table(H4_tax_rev$unit) #1000 obs each for million euros and percent of GDP
table(H4_tax_rev$na_item) #400 obs for each revenue source

#remove GDP unit because it is not needed
H4_tax_rev <- H4_tax_rev[H4_tax_rev$unit != "Percentage of gross domestic product (GDP)", ]
table(H4_tax_rev$unit) #only have million euro units
table(H4_tax_rev$geo) #100 obs for each country, which is correct
#remove sector column
H4_tax_rev$sector <- NULL

#reshape from long to wide format to create separate columns for each revenue source
H4_tax_rev <- reshape(
  H4_tax_rev,
  idvar = c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD"),
  timevar = "na_item",
  direction = "wide"
)

#remove unnecessary columns
H4_tax_rev <- H4_tax_rev[, -c(5,7,9,11)]

colnames(H4_tax_rev)[c(3,4,5,6,7,8)] <- c('tax.unit', 'tax.rev.production.imports',
                                        'tax.rev.income.wealth', 'tax.rev.net.social.cont',
                                        'tax.rev.captial', 'tax.rev.total')

#calculate YoY change in indirect taxes as a percentage of total taxes
#reorder rows
H4_tax_rev <- H4_tax_rev[order(H4_tax_rev$geo, H4_tax_rev$TIME_PERIOD), ]

#calculate indirect tax share
H4_tax_rev$indirect.share <- H4_tax_rev$tax.rev.production.imports / 
  (H4_tax_rev$tax.rev.production.imports + H4_tax_rev$tax.rev.income.wealth + 
     H4_tax_rev$tax.rev.net.social.cont + H4_tax_rev$tax.rev.captial)

#calculate yoy
H4_tax_rev$yoy.indirect.share <- ave(H4_tax_rev$indirect.share, H4_tax_rev$geo, FUN = function(x) {
  return(c(NA, diff(x)))
})

#multiply yoy change by 100
H4_tax_rev$yoy.indirect.share <- H4_tax_rev$yoy.indirect.share * 100

#rename Germany to "Germany" since it is named "Germany Linked" in dataset
H4_potential_gdp$geo[grepl("Germany", H4_potential_gdp$geo)] <- "Germany"
table(H4_potential_gdp$geo)

#reshape potential GDP dataset from long to wide
table(H4_potential_gdp$unit)
table(H4_potential_gdp$ITEM)

H4_potential_gdp <- reshape(
  H4_potential_gdp,
  idvar = c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD"),
  timevar = "ITEM",
  direction = "wide"
)

#remove unnecessary columns
H4_potential_gdp <- H4_potential_gdp[, -c(6)]

colnames(H4_potential_gdp)[c(3,4,5,6,7)] <- c('potential.unit', 'potential.output.gap',
                                        'potential.output.gap.units', 'potential.raw.values',
                                        'potential.currencies')

#the potential raw values are in euros, and the notation is for historical
#conversion reasons from the initial euro conversion from national currencies.
# euro_lock_rates <- c(
#   "Austria"     = 13.7603,
#   "Belgium"     = 40.3399,
#   "Finland"     = 5.94573,
#   "France"      = 6.55957,
#   "Germany"     = 1.95583,
#   "Greece"      = 340.750,
#   "Italy"       = 1936.27,
#   "Netherlands" = 2.20371,
#   "Portugal"    = 200.482,
#   "Spain"       = 166.386
# )
#however, the raw values are in millarden, which is German for billion, so to
#match the units of tax revenue, which are in millions of euros, I just need to
#multiply the raw potential values by 1000.
H4_potential_gdp$potential.gdp.eur.millions <- H4_potential_gdp$potential.raw.values * 1000

#subset each dataset to isolate total tax revenue and potential GDP values
total_tax_rev <- subset(H4_tax_rev, select = c(1,2,3,8))
potential_GDP <- subset(H4_potential_gdp, select = c(1,2,8))

#merge the subsets
H4_gov_revenue <- merge(total_tax_rev, 
                     potential_GDP, 
                     by = c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD"), 
                     all = TRUE)

table(H4_gov_revenue$geo) #20 observations for each country 2000-2019

#calculate YoY chnages in total tax revenue as a percentage of potential GDP
H4_gov_revenue <- H4_gov_revenue[order(H4_gov_revenue$geo, H4_gov_revenue$TIME_PERIOD), ]
H4_gov_revenue$tax.revenue.pct.gdp <- (H4_gov_revenue$tax.rev.total / H4_gov_revenue$potential.gdp.eur.millions) * 100
#calculate YoY change
H4_gov_revenue$yoy.tax.revenue.pct.gdp <- ave(
  H4_gov_revenue$tax.revenue.pct.gdp, 
  H4_gov_revenue$geo, 
  FUN = function(x) { return(c(NA, diff(x))) }
)


#reshape H4_social from long to wide
table(H4_social$cofog99) #have health and social protection spending, 400 obs each
table(H4_social$unit) #have millions of euros and percent of GDP, 400 obs each

#remove percent of GDP units
H4_social <- H4_social[H4_social$unit != "Percentage of gross domestic product (GDP)", ]
table(H4_social$unit) #400 obs, which is correct
table(H4_social$geo) #40 obs per country, which is correct
table(H4_social$cofog99) #200 obs each, which is correct

H4_social <- reshape(
  H4_social,
  idvar = c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD"),    
  timevar = "cofog99",      
  v.names = "OBS_VALUE",    
  direction = "wide"              
)

table(H4_social$geo) #20 obs each, which is correct

#to calculate YoY change in social protection expenditure as a percentage of 
#potential GDP, we must do the same thing we did with total tax revenue and 
#subset both datasets, merge them, and then calculate the values. Since the 
#potential GDP dataset is already isolated, only have to subset the H4_social data
social_protection <- subset(H4_social, select = c(1,2,3,6,7))

#merge the subsets
H4_social_expenditure <- merge(social_protection, 
                        potential_GDP, 
                        by = c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD"), 
                        all = TRUE)

table(H4_social_expenditure$geo) #20 observations for each country 2000-2019
#rename unit column
names(H4_social_expenditure)[c(3,4,5)] <- c('social.unit', 'social.health.exp', 'social.protection.exp')
H4_social_expenditure <- H4_social_expenditure[order(H4_social_expenditure$geo, H4_social_expenditure$TIME_PERIOD), ]
#add health and social protection exp together to get overall social expenditure
H4_social_expenditure$total.social.welfare <- rowSums(
  H4_social_expenditure[, c("social.health.exp", "social.protection.exp")], 
  na.rm = TRUE
)
#calculate YoY changes in total social exp as a percentage of potential GDP
H4_social_expenditure <- H4_social_expenditure[order(H4_social_expenditure$geo, H4_social_expenditure$TIME_PERIOD), ]
H4_social_expenditure$social.exp.pct.gdp <- (H4_social_expenditure$total.social.welfare / H4_social_expenditure$potential.gdp.eur.millions) * 100
#calculate YoY change
H4_social_expenditure$yoy.social.exp.pct.gdp <- ave(
  H4_social_expenditure$social.exp.pct.gdp, 
  H4_social_expenditure$geo, 
  FUN = function(x) { return(c(NA, diff(x))) }
)

#calculate YoY change in output gap
H4_potential_gdp$yoy.output.gap <- ave(
  H4_potential_gdp$potential.output.gap, 
  H4_potential_gdp$geo, 
  FUN = function(x) { return(c(NA, diff(x))) }
)
table(H4_potential_gdp$geo)
sum(is.na(H4_potential_gdp)) #10 NA values which is correct from YoY calcs for 10 countries

#calculate YoY change in total unemployment
H4_unemployment$yoy.unemployment <- ave(
  H4_unemployment$OBS_VALUE, 
  H4_unemployment$geo, 
  FUN = function(x) { return(c(NA, diff(x))) }
)
table(H4_unemployment$geo)
sum(is.na(H4_unemployment)) #10 NA values which is correct from YoY calcs


#_______________________________________________________________________________

#clean up datasets needed for analysis and get all variables in one place

#_______________________________________________________________________________


#H4_inequality_ratio names
names(H4_inequality_ratio)[c(3,4,7)] <- c('inequality.unit', 'inequality.obs.value', 'inequality.yoy.change')
#remove unnecessary columns. Note that contain total sex and total age
H4_inequality_ratio <- subset(H4_inequality_ratio, select = -c(5,6))
table(H4_inequality_ratio$geo)
#Germany is correctly named

#H4_tax_rev
#rename dataset to be a better name
H4_indirect_tax_share <- H4_tax_rev
#remove unnecessary columns
H4_indirect_tax_share <- subset(H4_indirect_tax_share, select = c(1,2,3,9,10))
names(H4_indirect_tax_share)[3] <- ('indirect.unit')
table(H4_indirect_tax_share$geo)
#Germany is correctly named

#H4_gov_revenue
#remove unnecessary columns
H4_gov_revenue <- subset(H4_gov_revenue, select = c(1,2,3,6,7))
table(H4_gov_revenue$geo)
#Germany is correctly named

#H4_social_expenditure
#remove unnecessary columns
H4_social_expenditure <- subset(H4_social_expenditure, select = c(1,2,3,7,8,9))
table(H4_social_expenditure$geo)
#Germany is correctly named

#H4_output_gap
H4_output_gap <- subset(H4_potential_gdp, select = c(1,2,4,5,9))
table(H4_output_gap$geo)
#Germany is correctly named

#H4_unemployment
#remove unnecessary columns. Note that unemployment includes total sex, age, and citizen
H4_unemployment <- subset(H4_unemployment, select = c(1,2,3,4,8))
names(H4_unemployment)[c(3,4,5)] <- c('unemployment.unit', 'unemployment.obs.value',
                                      'unemployment.yoy.change')
table(H4_unemployment$geo)
#Germany is correctly named

#H4_real_GDP
#remove unnecessary columns.
H4_real_GDP_growth$unit <- NULL
names(H4_real_GDP_growth)[c(3,4)] <- c('real.gdp.yoy.change', 'real.gdp.unit')
table(H4_real_GDP_growth$geo)
#Germany is correctly named
```

## EDA ON HYPOTHESIS 4 DATASETS

```{r include=FALSE}
#EDA ON H4_inequality_ratio
nrow(H4_inequality_ratio)
#168 observations
table(H4_inequality_ratio$geo) #all countries have 17 observations, except 
#Germany and Netherlands with 16 obs
table(H4_inequality_ratio$TIME_PERIOD) #missing 2 obs from 2004 
sum(is.na(H4_inequality_ratio)) #10 missing values, which is correct for YoY calcs

str(H4_inequality_ratio)
summary(H4_inequality_ratio$inequality.yoy.change)
#min value is -0.6, median is -0.01, mean is -0.0016, max is 0.87
colSums(is.na(H4_inequality_ratio))
#only missing values in the yoy columns, 10 each

#remove rows with NA values
H4_inequality_ratio <- na.omit(H4_inequality_ratio)
table(H4_inequality_ratio$geo)
#now have 16 observations per country, except for Germany and Netherlands with 15 obs
sum(is.na(H4_inequality_ratio)) #no missing values
par(mfrow=c(1,1))
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
boxplot(inequality.yoy.change ~ geo,
        data=H4_inequality_ratio,
        main='H4 YoY Change in Inequality Ratio',
        ylab='YoY change in inequality ratio',
        xlab='country')
```

Surprisingly, Germany has the most variability compared to the other countries.
Germany also has the largest positive whisker out of all included countries, 
indicating wealth inequality widens more than it narrows given its negative whisker
is small. The remaining core Eurozone countries all have relatively limited variability
compared to Germany and the periphery countries. Italy, Greece, and Spain all have
more or less equal whiskers in both directions. Greece and Portugal both have 
significant median values below 0 indicating a reduction in income inequality.
The Netherlands has a slightly positive median whereas the remaining countries
are all near 0. Greece has two outliers, one positive and one negative. France has
a large positive outlier, possibly from the crisis years. 

```{r include=FALSE}
#EDA ON H4_indirect_tax_share
nrow(H4_indirect_tax_share) 
#200 observations
table(H4_indirect_tax_share$geo) 
#20 obs for each country
sum(is.na(H4_indirect_tax_share))
#missing 10 values from YoY calcs
str(H4_indirect_tax_share)
summary(H4_indirect_tax_share$yoy.indirect.share)
#min value is -3.35, median is 0.018, mean is 0.065, max is 4.376
colSums(is.na(H4_indirect_tax_share))
#10 missing values in YoY column

#remove 2000 rows because they have NA values from the YoY change calc
H4_indirect_tax_share <- H4_indirect_tax_share[H4_indirect_tax_share$TIME_PERIOD != 2000, ]
table(H4_indirect_tax_share$geo)
colSums(is.na(H4_indirect_tax_share))
#now have 19 observations per country, which is correct, and no missing values
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
boxplot(yoy.indirect.share ~ geo,
        data=H4_indirect_tax_share,
        main='H4 YoY Change in Indirect Tax Share',
        ylab = 'percentage of potential GDP',
        xlab='country')
```

The core Eurozone countries have little variability compared to the periphery 
countries. All core countries do have outliers, except for Belgium. Austria, France, 
and Germany all have positive outliers, with Austria and Germany having 3 each.
The periphery countries have larger whiskers and quartiles, however these countries
are evenly spread from their means despite the increased variability. Spain and 
the Netherlands both have very large positive outliers, indicating that for one 
year indirect tax revenue rose sharply. All median values are near 0.

```{r include=FALSE}
#EDA ON H4_gov_revenue
nrow(H4_gov_revenue)
#200 observations
table(H4_gov_revenue$geo)
#every country has 20 observations, which is correct
sum(is.na(H4_gov_revenue$yoy.tax.revenue.pct.gdp))
#missing 10 values each, which is because we eliminate 2002 observations when
#calculating yoy and only have 10 countries instead of 11 
str(H4_gov_revenue)
summary(H4_gov_revenue$yoy.tax.revenue.pct.gdp)
#min value is -3.97, median is 0.757, mean is 0.67, max is 3.2
colSums(is.na(H4_gov_revenue))
#only missing values in the yoy columns, 10 total, one for each country

#remove 2000 rows because they have NA values from the YoY change calc
H4_gov_revenue <- H4_gov_revenue[H4_gov_revenue$TIME_PERIOD != 2000, ]
table(H4_gov_revenue$geo)
colSums(is.na(H4_gov_revenue))
#now have 19 observations per country, which is correct, and no missing values
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
boxplot(yoy.tax.revenue.pct.gdp ~ geo,
        data=H4_gov_revenue,
        main='H4 YoY Change in General Governemnt Tax Revenue',
        ylab= "percent of potential GDP",
        xlab='country')
```

All countries have similar variance, with Finland, Greece, and Portugal all having 
the largest variance. All median values are around 1%, indicating relatively 
constant tax revenue. However, there are significant outliers for most countries.
Spain has the largest negative outliers. Italy surprisingly has the tightest outliers.
Germany and Austria are the only two countries to not have outliers.

```{r include=FALSE}
#EDA ON H4_social_expenditure
nrow(H4_social_expenditure)
#200 observations
table(H4_social_expenditure$geo)
#every country has 20 observations, which is correct
sum(is.na(H4_social_expenditure))
colSums(is.na(H4_social_expenditure))
#no missing values except for YoY change, which is 10
str(H4_social_expenditure)
summary(H4_social_expenditure$yoy.social.exp.pct.gdp)
#min value is -2.386, median is 0.4897, mean is 0.51, max is 2.38

#remove 2000 rows because they have NA values
H4_social_expenditure <- H4_social_expenditure[H4_social_expenditure$TIME_PERIOD != 2000, ]
table(H4_social_expenditure$geo)
colSums(is.na(H4_social_expenditure))
#now have 19 observations per country, which is correct and no missing values
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
boxplot(yoy.social.exp.pct.gdp ~ geo,
        data=H4_social_expenditure,
        main='H4 YoY Change in Social Expenditure',
        ylab= 'percent of potential GDP',
        xlab='country')
```

Greece has by far the largest variability compared to all of the other Eurozone
countries, including the periphery countries. All median values are around 0.05.
France has the tightest IQR indicating they did not increase or decrease 
overall social expenditures. The core Eurozone countries are also relatively tight,
including Italy which is surprising. Greece has a large negative outlier, which 
makes sense considering they were forced to cut government spending during the crisis.
Portugal and Spain both have large positive outliers which is surprising considering
they were most impacted by the debt crisis.

```{r include=FALSE}
#EDA ON H4_output_gap
nrow(H4_output_gap)
#200 observations
table(H4_output_gap$geo)
#every country has 20 observations, which is correct
sum(is.na(H4_output_gap$yoy.output.gap))
#10 missing values because I removed observation from 2000 when calculating YoY 
str(H4_output_gap)
summary(H4_output_gap$yoy.output.gap)
#min value is -8.596, median is 0.207, mean is -0.071, max is 3.603
colSums(is.na(H4_output_gap))
#only missing values in the yoy columns, 10 each

#remove 2000 rows because they have NA values
H4_output_gap <- H4_output_gap[H4_output_gap$TIME_PERIOD != 2000, ]
table(H4_output_gap$geo)
colSums(is.na(H4_output_gap))
#now have 19 observations per country, which is correct and no missing values
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
boxplot(yoy.output.gap ~ geo,
        data=H4_output_gap,
        main='H4 YoY Change in Output Gap',
        ylab = 'percent of potential GDP',
        xlab='country')
```

All median values with the exception of Spain are near 0. Spain is around -1. 
The IQR of all countries is similar, except for Greece and Finland which have more
variability. Germany and Spain have the largest positive whiskers and Greece has
the largest negative whiskers. All countries except Portugal have large negative 
outliers, most likely during the crisis years. It is surprising that Portugal does 
not have this as well. Finland has the largest negative outlier followed by Germany.

```{r include=FALSE}
#EDA ON H4_unemployment
nrow(H4_unemployment)
#200 observations
table(H4_unemployment$geo)
#every country has 20 observations, which is correct because I started at 2000
sum(is.na(H4_unemployment))
#10 missing values
colSums(is.na(H4_unemployment))
#10 missing values in yoy column
str(H4_unemployment)
summary(H4_unemployment$unemployment.yoy.change)
#min value is -3.4, median is -0.25, mean is -0.02, max is 6.60

#remove 2000 rows because they have NA values
H4_unemployment <- H4_unemployment[H4_unemployment$TIME_PERIOD != 2000, ]
table(H4_unemployment$geo)
colSums(is.na(H4_unemployment))
#10 observations per country and no missing values
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
boxplot(unemployment.yoy.change ~ geo,
        data=H4_unemployment,
        main='H4 YoY Change in Total Unemployment',
        ylab= 'percent from previous period',
        xlab='country')
```

All core Eurozone countries have low variability compared to the periphery countries.
Greece, Spain, and Portugal all have the largest unemployment variability, with 
Greece and Spain having the largest positive outliers. Germany and France also 
have one positive outlier each, but it is not large. All median values are around
0, except Portugal which is near 1.

```{r include=FALSE}
#EDA ON H4_real_GDP_growth
nrow(H4_real_GDP_growth)
#200 observations
table(H4_real_GDP_growth$geo)
#every country has 20 observations, which is correct because I started at 2000
sum(is.na(H4_real_GDP_growth))
#no missing values
colSums(is.na(H4_real_GDP_growth))
#no missing values in yoy column
str(H4_real_GDP_growth)
summary(H4_real_GDP_growth$real.gdp.yoy.change)
#min value is -9.9, median is 1.650, mean is 1.316, max is 6.40
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
boxplot(real.gdp.yoy.change ~ geo,
        data=H4_real_GDP_growth,
        main='H4 YoY Change in Real GDP Growth',
        ylab= 'percent from previous period',
        xlab='country')
```

The core Eurozone countries have relatively tight box plots, with Germany showing
slightly more variability in its IQR. Greece, Spain, and Portugal all have the 
largest variability out of the Eurozone countries, with Greece having by far the 
most. These three countries all have large negative whiskers. All countries except
for Spain and Portugal have large negative outliers. Finland and Germany have the 
largest outliers from their end whiskers. Greece has the largest outlier from its
median value. All median values are around 2% for the core Eurozone countries.
Italy has a median value around 1% and Spain is around 3%.

```{r include=FALSE}
#_______________________________________________________________________________

#H4 MERGE ALL DATASETS

#_______________________________________________________________________________


#recheck all datasets
nrow(H4_inequality_ratio) #158
nrow(H4_indirect_tax_share) #190
nrow(H4_gov_revenue) #190
nrow(H4_social_expenditure) #190
nrow(H4_output_gap) #190
nrow(H4_unemployment) #190
nrow(H4_real_GDP_growth) #200

table(H4_inequality_ratio$geo)
table(H4_indirect_tax_share$geo)
table(H4_gov_revenue$geo)
table(H4_social_expenditure$geo)
table(H4_output_gap$geo) 
table(H4_unemployment$geo)
table(H4_real_GDP_growth$geo)
#all datasets have the same 10 countries 

#MERGE ALL H4 DATASETS

#make list of datasets to merge
dataset_list <- list(H4_inequality_ratio, H4_indirect_tax_share, H4_gov_revenue,
                     H4_social_expenditure, H4_output_gap, H4_unemployment, H4_real_GDP_growth)

H4_final <- Reduce(function(x, y) merge(x, y, by = c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD"), all = TRUE), 
                   dataset_list)

nrow(H4_final)
#210 rows
table(H4_final$geo)
#all countries have 21 observation, including NA values. This is because the 
#dependent variable goes until 2020 and the predictor variables start at 2000.


#______________________________________________________________________________

#H4 LAG NECESSARY VARIABLES IN FINAL DATASET

#_______________________________________________________________________________
```

## HYPOTHESIS 4 PREDICTOR LAGS

The Dependent Variable (S80/S20 Income Quintile Share Ratio) requires a structural
1-year adjustment due to the data gathering design of the EU-SILC survey. Because
the survey is conducted in year t but measures actual household income from year t-1,
a value recorded in 2005 actually reflects 2004 inequality. Therefore, variables that
econometrically function as a "1-year lag" relative to true income must be lagged by
2 years (t-2) relative to the survey row. Variables functioning as "concurrent" to true
income must be lagged by 1 year (t-1) relative to the survey row.

Tax Mix (Indirect/Total Tax) and Structural Volatility are lagged by 2 years (t-2).
This accounts for both the 1-year survey delay and an intentional 1-year economic
transmission gap. Shifts in tax composition do not alter inequality overnight; it 
takes several quarters for regressive VAT hikes to systematically drain lower-class 
disposable income, alter consumption bundles, and widen the final rich-poor gap. 
Furthermore, tax shares are endogenous to current economic conditions. Lagging the 
tax variables by 2 years ensures we isolate the impact of past structural policy 
adjustments on subsequent inequality, eliminating reverse causality.

Total General Government Revenue Volume is lagged by 2 years (t-2) for identical
reasons. Raw tax and non-tax volumes are highly reactive to immediate macroeconomic 
shocks. By using potential GDP as the denominator and lagging the variable by 2 years,
we hold the sheer scale of historical fiscal consolidation constant, ensuring the 
tax mix coefficient purely measures the distributional composition effect rather
than the volume effect.

Social Protection Expenditure is lagged by 2 years (t-2). Welfare adjustments,
unemployment benefit changes, and healthcare cuts require time to pass through 
administrative pipelines and manifest as measurable changes in household income 
inequality. Lagging this variable ensures that the tax mix coefficient does not
inadvertently absorb the impact of simultaneous austerity cuts on the spending side 
of the same national budget.

Output Gap and Total Unemployment are lagged by 2 years (t-2). These act as
macroeconomic control baselines, tracking business cycle momentum and labor market 
shocks associated with the timing of the fiscal consolidation itself. Unemployment 
spikes in year t-2 dictate downstream income drops and layoffs that crystallize into 
the income distribution data recorded in year t-1 and ultimately surveyed in year t.

Real GDP Growth Rate is lagged by 1 year (t-1). This acts as a concurrent baseline
relative to the actual income year (t-1). Economic growth, aggregate demand, and 
macroeconomic momentum dictate employment opportunities and market-driven income 
distribution in real time. Leaving this unlagged relative to the true income year 
ensures we hold contemporaneous macroeconomic health constant, cleanly isolating 
whether the structural tax choices made the prior year actively worsened the rich-poor
gap independent of broader growth trends.

In summary, the model employs a deliberate operational asymmetry. By lagging the 
structural fiscal adjustments (tax mix, volume, and welfare) and business cycle 
shocks by 2 years, the model accounts for the survey reporting delay, protects 
against reverse causality, and captures the 1-year behavioral transmission gap 
required for structural policy choices to fragment household income. Conversely, 
by keeping real GDP growth concurrent to the true income year (1-year survey lag), 
the model establishes a strict baseline of the economy's contemporaneous performance. 
This directly serves the core goal: proving whether balancing a budget via consumer
sales taxes hurts economic equality more than targeting top-earner income taxes.

```{r include=FALSE}
#ensure that rows are in the exact linear sequence required for lagging.
H4_final <- H4_final[order(H4_final$geo, H4_final$TIME_PERIOD), ]

#define the custom panel lag function
panel_lag <- function(x, n, geo_vector) {
  ave(x, geo_vector, FUN = function(val) {
    if(length(val) <= n) return(rep(NA, length(val)))
    return(c(rep(NA, n), val[1:(length(val) - n)]))
  })
}

#create the operational lags relative to the survey year (t)
#for a macro event in 2003 to affect 2004 income, measured in a 2005 survey,
#it is a 2-year lag from the survey row.

H4_final$lag2.yoy.indirect.share  <- panel_lag(H4_final$yoy.indirect.share, 2, H4_final$geo)
H4_final$lag2.yoy.tax.rev.pct.gdp <- panel_lag(H4_final$yoy.tax.revenue.pct.gdp, 2, H4_final$geo)
H4_final$lag2.yoy.social.exp.pct.gdp  <- panel_lag(H4_final$yoy.social.exp.pct.gdp, 2, H4_final$geo)
H4_final$lag2.yoy.output.gap  <- panel_lag(H4_final$yoy.output.gap, 2, H4_final$geo)
H4_final$lag2.yoy.unemployment    <- panel_lag(H4_final$unemployment.yoy.change, 2, H4_final$geo)

#GDP growth is unlagged so only needs to be lagged 1 year in order to match up
#with the survey year
H4_final$lag1.yoy.real.gdp.growth <- panel_lag(H4_final$real.gdp.yoy.change, 1, H4_final$geo)

#check it worked
verification_fields <- c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD", "yoy.indirect.share", "lag2.yoy.indirect.share", 
                         "yoy.social.exp.pct.gdp", "lag2.yoy.social.exp.pct.gdp",
                         'real.gdp.yoy.change', 'lag1.yoy.real.gdp.growth')

print(H4_final[24:50, verification_fields], row.names = FALSE)
#everything looks good
```

## HYPOTHESIS 4 ASSUMPTION CHECKS

Our Hypothesis 4 model

```{r echo=TRUE}
H4_plm <- plm(inequality.yoy.change ~ lag2.yoy.indirect.share + lag2.yoy.tax.rev.pct.gdp + 
                lag2.yoy.social.exp.pct.gdp + lag2.yoy.output.gap + lag2.yoy.unemployment + 
                lag1.yoy.real.gdp.growth,
              data = H4_final,
              index = c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD"),
              model = "within", #within = fixed effects
              effect = "twoways")
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
#extract the within-transformed (demeaned) variables properly
transformed_X <- as.data.frame(model.matrix(H4_plm))
transformed_Y <- as.numeric(pmodel.response(H4_plm))

#bind them together into a temporary data frame
vif_data <- cbind(transformed_Y, transformed_X)

#run standard lm() because R now sees them as completely separate columns
pure_vif_check <- lm(transformed_Y ~ ., data = vif_data)
```

### MULTICOLLINEARITY CHECK

```{r echo=FALSE}
vif(pure_vif_check)
# > vif(pure_vif_check)
# lag2.yoy.indirect.share    lag2.yoy.tax.rev.pct.gdp lag2.yoy.social.exp.pct.gdp 
# 1.145087                    1.242014                    1.275046 
# lag2.yoy.output.gap       lag2.yoy.unemployment    lag1.yoy.real.gdp.growth 
# 1.667855                    1.897408                    1.410537  
```

All predictors have very low multicollinearity. This was done by treating 
the VIF check the same way the plm model handles the data, resulting in the 
isolated variations in each predictor.

### LINEARITY CHECK

```{r include=FALSE}
#extract the clean, model-transformed dependent variable (excludes dropped rows)
actual_Y <- as.numeric(pmodel.response(H4_plm))

#extract the model residuals
resids <- as.numeric(residuals(H4_plm))

#calculate fitted values using matching dimensions
fitted_values <- actual_Y - resids
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
plot(fitted_values, resids, 
     main = "H4 ex Ireland - Residuals vs Fitted", 
     xlab = "Fitted Values", ylab = "Residuals",
     pch = 19, col = rgb(0, 0, 0, 0.5))
abline(h = 0, col = "red", lwd = 2)

#LOESS smooth trend line (Blue, solid
lines(lowess(fitted_values, resids, f=0.75), col = "blue", lwd = 2)
```

All residuals are evenly scattered above and below 0. Our blue LOESS trend line is 
straight with no curve and stays close to 0 throughout the whole x-axis. There is 
no shape or pattern in the residuals. There are some residuals that have 
much higher variance, so homoscedasticity may not be met, but it is only for a
small amount of residuals, as most residuals are contained between +/-0.4 on 
the y-axis. I will run a formal Breusch-Pagan test to check homoscedasticity and
apply Driscoll-Kraay standard errors regardless of the results to further protect
my model's standard errors.
As for the spread on the x-axis, there are some outlier fitted values on the right 
and left, mostly the right, but it is only a handful of values. I will create 
a residual vs leverage plot to make sure these points are not strongly influencing 
my regression model.
Overall, I will say linearity is met.

### INDEPENDENCE CHECK

Test for Autocorrelation within countries
H0: errors are independent (no autocorrelation)
H1: errors are not independent (autocorrelation exists)

```{r echo=FALSE}
pbgtest(H4_plm) #Breusch-Godfrey test for panel data
# > pbgtest(H4_plm) #Breusch-Godfrey test for panel data
# 
# Breusch-Godfrey/Wooldridge test for serial correlation in panel models
# 
# data:  inequality.yoy.change ~ lag2.yoy.indirect.share + lag2.yoy.tax.rev.pct.gdp +  ...
# chisq = 23.69, df = 15, p-value = 0.07054
# alternative hypothesis: serial correlation in idiosyncratic errors
```

With a significance level of 0.05 and a p-value of 0.0705, we fail to reject H0 
and conclude that our errors are independent and do not suffer from autocorrelation.

Test for Cross-Sectional Dependence (Spatial correlation between countries)
H0: errors are independent
H1: errors are not independent

```{r echo=FALSE}
pcdtest(H4_plm, test = "lm")
# > pcdtest(H4_plm, test = "lm")
# 
# Breusch-Pagan LM test for cross-sectional dependence in panels
# 
# data:  inequality.yoy.change ~ lag2.yoy.indirect.share + lag2.yoy.tax.rev.pct.gdp +     lag2.yoy.social.exp.pct.gdp + lag2.yoy.output.gap + lag2.yoy.unemployment +     lag1.yoy.real.gdp.growth
# chisq = 56.999, df = 45, p-value = 0.1082
# alternative hypothesis: cross-sectional dependence
```

With a significance level of 0.05 and a p-value of 0.1082, we fail to reject H0 
and conclude that errors are independent between countries.

Even though autocorrelation is not present within or between countries, we still 
apply Driscoll-Kraay standard errors as a safeguard. 

### NORMALITY CHECK

```{r echo=FALSE}
#extract clean numeric residuals
resids <- as.numeric(residuals(H4_plm))

qqnorm(resids, main = "H4 ex Ireland - QQ Plot of Panel Residuals",
       ylab='standardized residuals')
qqline(resids, col = "red")
```

Our QQ plot shows that the panel residuals track the theoretical normal 
distribution closely between the -1 to 1 quantiles. Beyond this, the plot 
slightly deviates from the theoretical quantiles but matches the overall slope
without creating a non-linear shape. Tails are shown on the ends, with an S-shape 
on the right side that indicates the presence of large residuals that deviate 
from a strict normal distribution. The heavy-tails are expected given the macroeconomic
scope of this project. 
However, with a total panel size of around 150 observations, the Central Limit Theorem
ensures that the distribution of our coefficient estimates is asymptotically normal. 
Therefore, despite the non-normal distributions present in the extreme tails of the 
raw residuals, we can confidently rely on our standard errors and final inferential 
results. 
Overall, while the strict assumption of residual normality is technically violated
in the tails due to predictable crisis-driven outliers, the asymptotic properties 
of our sample size render the model highly robust and statistically valid.

### EQUAL VARIANCE CHECK

H0: errors have equal variance
H1: errors do not have equal variance

```{r echo=FALSE}
bptest(H4_plm)
# > bptest(H4_plm)
# 
# studentized Breusch-Pagan test
# 
# data:  H4_plm
# BP = 6.7703, df = 6, p-value = 0.3426
```

Since out p-value is 0.3426, this is greater than our significance level of 0.05,
so we fail to reject H0 and conclude that our errors have equal variance. 
We implement Driscoll-Kraay standard errors to ensure that the final significance 
testing and confidence intervals for our expenditure and revenue predictors are 
statistically conservative.

### LEVERAGE AND COOK's DISTANCE CHECK EX IRELAND

```{r include=FALSE}
#reset row names to sequence from 1 to the total number of rows
rownames(H4_final) <- NULL
#use least squares dummy variable 
H4_lsdv <- lm(inequality.yoy.change ~ lag2.yoy.indirect.share + lag2.yoy.tax.rev.pct.gdp + 
                lag2.yoy.social.exp.pct.gdp + lag2.yoy.output.gap + lag2.yoy.unemployment + 
                lag1.yoy.real.gdp.growth + factor(geo) + factor(TIME_PERIOD), data = H4_final)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
```

```{r echo=FALSE}
#Residuals vs Leverage plot
plot(H4_lsdv, which = 5, main = "H4 ex Ireland - Residuals vs Leverage")

#Cook's Distance by Observation Index
cooks_vals <- cooks.distance(H4_lsdv)
plot(cooks_vals, type = "h", main = "Cook's Distance", ylab = "Cook's D Value")

# Add a horizontal line for the 4/n threshold
abline(h = 4 / nrow(H4_final), col = "red", lty = 2)

#find the country and year for our top leverage points
H4_final[c(92, 118, 120), c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD")]
# > H4_final[c(92, 118, 120), c("geo", "TIME_PERIOD")]
# geo TIME_PERIOD
# 92  Germany        2007
# 118  Greece        2012
# 120  Greece        2014
```

Observation 92 (Germany, 2007): This data point is a high residual, moderate leverage
outlier. Germany sits high on the vertical axis with a standardized residual approaching
+3.6 and leverage near 0.2, indicating that the YoY widening of the S80/S20
inequality ratio was significantly higher than what the model's predictors anticipated.
This clean vertical deviation captures the unmodeled distributional aftermath of
the sweeping Hartz IV welfare and low-wage labor market overhauls.

Observation 118 (Greece, 2012): This observation is a moderate residual (near 2.2),
high leverage (leverage approx. 0.35) outlier. As the peak of the Greek sovereign
debt crisis, 2012 was marked by unprecedented structural shocks: sudden, large
statutory VAT spikes implemented alongside collapsing output gaps and rising
unemployment. The model significantly underpredicted the velocity at which this
aggressive reliance on regressive indirect taxation would widen the rich-poor gap,
making this a vital observation for capturing acute policy-driven inequality.

Observation 120 (Greece, 2014): This data point is a high residual (-2.2),
high leverage (0.45). This reflects a highly unique policy environment where
the independent variables (sustained austerity revenues and depressed
macro fundamentals) reached an extreme. While its unique data composition pushes
the leverage baseline outward, it sits comfortably below the standard Cook's Distance
0.5 boundary line, confirming that its structural distance does not single-handedly
distort the global panel trend.

The Cook's Distance Plot highlights the sample's internal influence distribution,
peaking at a conservative maximum of just 0.12. Because our panel is focused cleanly
on core and periphery Eurozone dynamics, the vertical scale remains remarkably clean
and free of distortion.

The Case for Keeping Germany 2007: Retaining this outlier allows the model to absorb
an authentic structural shift in Europe's largest economy. Because its leverage is low,
it provides a clean, vertical data point that stress-tests the baseline predictive power
of ourmacroeconomic control variables without warping the regression line.

The Case for Keeping Greece 2012 and 2014: These observations represent authentic
institutional responses to extreme fiscal stress. Removing them would fundamentally
limit the model, masking the exact regressive inequality shocks that Hypothesis 4
seeks to evaluate. Their elevated leverage provides the necessary historical variation
in the tax mix variable (lag2.yoy.indirect.share) to cleanly prove whether flat
consumption tax hikes widen the rich-poor gap more aggressively than progressive
direct income taxes.

## HYPOTHESIS 4 INEQUALITY ANALYSIS

```{r include=FALSE}
#apply Driscoll-Kraay Standard Errors to H4 plm model
H4_final_results <- coeftest(H4_plm, 
                             vcov = vcovSCC(H4_plm, 
                                            type = "HC3", 
                                            cluster = "time"))
```

Null Hypothesis 4: Holding the total scale of fiscal consolidation constant (raising 
the exact same amount of total government revenue), the specific mix of taxation
